questions.question: Why did vertical scaling stop being a reliable answer for big-data workloads?
options: It eventually hit physical and financial limits
It only worked for Python jobs
It prevented SQL joins
It required streaming-only architectures
questions.correctAnswer: It eventually hit physical and financial limits
questions.explanation: Single-machine upgrades became too expensive and still could not keep up with exponential data growth.

questions.question: Why is veracity especially important in a smart-grid data platform?
options: Bad readings can trigger wrong operational decisions at scale
It removes the need for schema design
It guarantees every dataset fits in memory
It eliminates the need for feature engineering
questions.correctAnswer: Bad readings can trigger wrong operational decisions at scale
questions.explanation: At utility scale, missing or corrupted readings can lead to false alerts, bad forecasts, or even harmful automated actions.

questions.question: What made Spark fundamentally faster than classic MapReduce for iterative analytics?
options: It could keep working data in memory and avoid repeated disk writes
It automatically removed all shuffle operations
It always executed on GPUs
It stored every dataset as a single partition
questions.correctAnswer: It could keep working data in memory and avoid repeated disk writes
questions.explanation: Spark’s in-memory execution model reduces repeated disk I/O, which is the main reason it outperformed MapReduce on iterative workloads.

questions.question: What is the main role of `SparkSession` in a PySpark application?
options: It is the driver-side entry point that builds and coordinates plans
It stores all partitions directly on executors
It replaces the need for DataFrames
It writes output files automatically after every transformation
questions.correctAnswer: It is the driver-side entry point that builds and coordinates plans
questions.explanation: SparkSession lives on the driver and serves as the entry point for creating DataFrames and submitting work.

questions.question: What is the biggest risk of calling `collect()` on a very large dataset?
options: It can overwhelm driver memory
It disables the Catalyst optimizer
It forces Spark to write CSV output
It prevents lazy evaluation from working
questions.correctAnswer: It can overwhelm driver memory
questions.explanation: collect() moves data to the driver process, so it becomes dangerous when the result is too large to fit comfortably in driver memory.

questions.question: When reducing the number of output partitions before a write, which operation is usually cheaper?
options: coalesce()
repartition()
collect()
cache()
questions.correctAnswer: coalesce()
questions.explanation: coalesce() can often reduce partitions without paying for a full shuffle, making it the cheaper option in common write-down scenarios.

questions.question: In which Catalyst phase are rules like predicate pushdown and projection pruning applied?
options: Optimized logical plan
Unresolved logical plan
Python parsing stage
Shuffle write stage
questions.correctAnswer: Optimized logical plan
questions.explanation: Catalyst applies optimization rules after analysis, producing the optimized logical plan.

questions.question: What usually creates a new stage in Spark execution?
options: A shuffle boundary caused by a wide dependency
Every single transformation
Every executor heartbeat
Any call to printSchema()
questions.correctAnswer: A shuffle boundary caused by a wide dependency
questions.explanation: Spark splits stages where data must be redistributed across partitions, which is the hallmark of a wide dependency.

questions.question: If a stage has 500 partitions, how many tasks will Spark generally create for that stage?
options: It depends only on driver memory
questions.explanation: Spark commonly schedules one task per partition for a given stage.

questions.question: Which Spark UI metrics most directly reveal spill pressure?
options: Memory Bytes Spilled and Disk Bytes Spilled
Application name and Spark version
Executor CPU model and hostname
Rows Written and SQL text only
questions.correctAnswer: Memory Bytes Spilled and Disk Bytes Spilled
questions.explanation: Those metrics show that Spark had to spill intermediate data because memory pressure was too high.

questions.question: For a 1 TB fact table joined to a 5 MB dimension table, what is usually the best first optimization to try?
options: Broadcast the small dimension table
Force both tables into one partition
Disable adaptive execution
Call collect() on the dimension first
questions.correctAnswer: Broadcast the small dimension table
questions.explanation: Broadcasting the tiny side avoids shuffling the massive fact table and is often the highest-leverage first move.

questions.question: What is the goal of salting a skewed join key?
options: Split a hot key into multiple pseudo-keys so the workload spreads across partitions
Encrypt the join column before shuffling
Reduce every partition to one row
Guarantee that Spark chooses a broadcast join
questions.correctAnswer: Split a hot key into multiple pseudo-keys so the workload spreads across partitions
questions.explanation: Salting breaks up dominant keys so they no longer concentrate almost all work in a single partition.

questions.question: Which join type should you use to find rows from the left dataset that have no match on the right?
options: LEFT ANTI JOIN
INNER JOIN
CROSS JOIN
LEFT SEMI JOIN
questions.correctAnswer: LEFT ANTI JOIN
questions.explanation: A left anti join returns only the unmatched rows from the left side, which makes it useful for orphan detection and data quality checks.

questions.question: Why are window functions often preferable to self-joins for tasks like ranking and lagging within groups?
options: They often avoid the extra shuffle and duplication costs of self-joins
They force all rows into a single executor
They work only in local mode
They automatically cache every intermediate result
questions.correctAnswer: They often avoid the extra shuffle and duplication costs of self-joins
questions.explanation: Window functions can express per-group calculations directly, often with less data movement than a separate self-join pattern.

questions.question: When is a broadcast hash join a strong candidate?
options: When one side is small enough to fit in executor memory and broadcasting avoids shuffling the large side
When both sides are far too large to move over the network
Only when the join key is always unique
Only when using Python UDFs on the join condition
questions.correctAnswer: When one side is small enough to fit in executor memory and broadcasting avoids shuffling the large side
questions.explanation: Broadcast joins shine when one table is genuinely small, because Spark can send it to executors and keep the large table in place.

questions.question: What is the scope of `createOrReplaceTempView()`?
options: The current SparkSession
Every Spark cluster in the company
Only the current executor
Only notebook cells that run after a cache() call
questions.correctAnswer: The current SparkSession
questions.explanation: A temporary view created this way is visible inside the current SparkSession and disappears when that session ends.

questions.question: What does `spark.sql(...)` return?
options: A DataFrame
A Python list of rows
A JDBC connection
A permanent table definition
questions.correctAnswer: A DataFrame
questions.explanation: Spark SQL queries return DataFrames, so you can continue chaining DataFrame operations after a SQL statement.

questions.question: What is the key architectural relationship between Spark SQL and the DataFrame API?
options: They are two interfaces to the same engine and optimizer
SQL always bypasses Catalyst while DataFrames use it
DataFrames can run on executors but SQL can only run on the driver
SQL is faster only because it disables Tungsten
questions.correctAnswer: They are two interfaces to the same engine and optimizer
questions.explanation: Both Spark SQL and DataFrames converge on Catalyst and the same physical execution machinery.

questions.question: In the medallion architecture, which layer should contain cleaned, standardized, analytics-ready base records?
options: Silver
Bronze
Gold
Archive
questions.correctAnswer: Silver
questions.explanation: Silver is where raw data is cleaned, typed, deduplicated, and standardized before business-ready modeling happens in gold.

questions.question: What is a common distributed pattern for deterministic deduplication when you want to keep the latest record per business key?
options: Use `row_number()` over a partitioned window ordered by recency, then keep rank 1
Use `collect()` and deduplicate locally on the driver
Sort the whole dataset into one partition and overwrite it
Replace every duplicate with NULL values
questions.correctAnswer: Use `row_number()` over a partitioned window ordered by recency, then keep rank 1
questions.explanation: A windowed row_number pattern is a standard, scalable way to keep the latest or best row per key.

questions.question: What should a quality gate do when a transformed dataset fails critical validation rules?
options: Block promotion to the next layer and surface the failure clearly
Silently write the bad data to gold anyway
Retry forever until the rules pass automatically
Delete the raw data that caused the failure
questions.correctAnswer: Block promotion to the next layer and surface the failure clearly
questions.explanation: A quality gate exists to stop bad data from contaminating downstream layers while making the failure visible for investigation.

questions.question: What does `explode()` do to an array column?
options: Turns each element into its own row
Converts the array into a map
Drops the array column entirely
Sends the array back to the driver
questions.correctAnswer: Turns each element into its own row
questions.explanation: explode() expands one array-valued row into many rows, one per element.

questions.question: What is a Spark `struct` best understood as?
options: A single column that groups named nested fields together
A storage format that replaces Parquet
A table-level partitioning strategy
A command that caches every row in memory
questions.correctAnswer: A single column that groups named nested fields together
questions.explanation: Structs model nested records, letting one column contain multiple named fields such as address.city and address.zip.

questions.question: What is a major risk when exploding nested collections too early in a pipeline?
options: You can multiply row counts dramatically and create unnecessary shuffle cost
Spark stops supporting schema evolution
Executors can no longer read JSON
The DataFrame API becomes SQL-only
questions.correctAnswer: You can multiply row counts dramatically and create unnecessary shuffle cost
questions.explanation: Exploding arrays or maps too early can blow up the dataset size, which then makes every downstream operation more expensive.

questions.question: Why should you prefer built-in Spark functions over Python UDFs when possible?
options: Built-ins stay inside Spark’s optimized engine and avoid Python serialization overhead
Python UDFs can only run on the driver
Built-ins are the only functions allowed in notebooks
Python UDFs always disable partitioning
questions.correctAnswer: Built-ins stay inside Spark’s optimized engine and avoid Python serialization overhead
questions.explanation: Built-in expressions remain visible to Catalyst and avoid the Python/JVM crossing costs that make UDFs slower.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of a pandas UDF compared with a row-at-a-time Python UDF?
options: It processes data in vectorized Arrow-backed batches
It removes the need for executors
It guarantees zero serialization cost
It only works on a single partition at a time
questions.correctAnswer: It processes data in vectorized Arrow-backed batches
questions.explanation: pandas UDFs use vectorized batches and Arrow, which makes them much more efficient than one-row-at-a-time Python UDFs.

questions.question: When is a grouped map UDF justified?
options: When you need custom per-group logic that built-in functions cannot express cleanly
Whenever a simple `groupBy().agg()` is available
Only when the dataset fits on the driver
Only for joins with broadcast hints
questions.correctAnswer: When you need custom per-group logic that built-in functions cannot express cleanly
questions.explanation: Grouped map UDFs are a more expensive escape hatch, so they are best reserved for genuinely custom per-group logic.

questions.question: What makes a dataset “unbounded” in Structured Streaming?
options: New records keep arriving continuously over time
It has more than one partition
It cannot be written to Parquet
It uses SQL instead of DataFrames
questions.correctAnswer: New records keep arriving continuously over time
questions.explanation: An unbounded dataset has no fixed final size because events continue to arrive.

questions.question: Why is a checkpoint location essential in Structured Streaming?
options: It stores offsets and state so the stream can recover correctly after failures
It converts the stream into a batch job
It permanently disables watermarking
It avoids the need for output sinks
questions.correctAnswer: It stores offsets and state so the stream can recover correctly after failures
questions.explanation: Checkpoints preserve the stream’s progress and state so restarts can resume safely instead of duplicating or losing work.

questions.question: When is append mode a good fit for a streaming sink?
options: When emitted rows will not need to be revised later
When every aggregation must update historical output rows repeatedly
When the sink cannot accept any new data after startup
When you want to materialize a global sort every micro-batch
questions.correctAnswer: When emitted rows will not need to be revised later
questions.explanation: Append mode works best when records can be written once and left alone, rather than updated after more late data arrives.

questions.question: What does Delta Lake add on top of a regular data lake?
options: ACID transactions and reliable table semantics
A requirement to store everything as CSV
Automatic GPU acceleration
A ban on schema evolution
questions.correctAnswer: ACID transactions and reliable table semantics
questions.explanation: Delta Lake brings transactionality, consistency, and other database-like guarantees to lake storage.

questions.question: Which Delta Lake command pattern is commonly used for upserts?
options: MERGE
collect()
cache()
describe()
questions.correctAnswer: MERGE
questions.explanation: MERGE lets you match existing rows and then update or insert as needed, which is the core upsert pattern in Delta Lake.

questions.question: What does Delta Lake time travel allow you to do?
options: Query an earlier version of the table
Recover deleted driver logs only
Broadcast a table automatically to every executor
Convert all historical files into a single partition
questions.correctAnswer: Query an earlier version of the table
questions.explanation: Time travel lets you inspect or restore earlier table states, which is useful for debugging, audits, and rollback workflows.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a unit test in a data pipeline codebase?
options: Validate a small transformation or helper in isolation
Benchmark an entire production cluster
Replace observability and alerting
Guarantee that source data is always clean
questions.correctAnswer: Validate a small transformation or helper in isolation
questions.explanation: Unit tests target focused logic so bugs can be caught quickly without needing the whole pipeline to run.

questions.question: What does an integration test usually add beyond unit tests in a Spark project?
options: It exercises multiple pipeline pieces together against realistic data slices
It proves that no schema will ever change
It removes the need for production monitoring
It guarantees zero runtime cost
questions.correctAnswer: It exercises multiple pipeline pieces together against realistic data slices
questions.explanation: Integration tests verify that several components work together, often in a local Spark runtime with representative inputs.

questions.question: What does observability give you that a one-time test suite cannot?
options: Continuous visibility into freshness, volume, failures, and anomalies in production
A guarantee that every future schema change is harmless
Automatic elimination of all bad source data
A way to avoid writing alerts entirely
questions.correctAnswer: Continuous visibility into freshness, volume, failures, and anomalies in production
questions.explanation: Tests validate expected behavior ahead of time; observability tells you what is happening in the live system right now.

questions.question: In `spark-submit --deploy-mode cluster`, where does the driver run?
options: Inside the cluster
Only on your laptop
Inside every executor at once
In the metadata catalog
questions.correctAnswer: Inside the cluster
questions.explanation: Cluster mode moves the driver off the submitting machine and into the cluster environment.

questions.question: What is an Airflow DAG primarily used to model?
options: Task dependencies, scheduling, and retries across a workflow
The physical layout of parquet files on disk
Python virtual environment resolution only
Spark executor JVM memory pages
questions.correctAnswer: Task dependencies, scheduling, and retries across a workflow
questions.explanation: An Airflow DAG describes what tasks exist, how they depend on each other, and how they should be scheduled and retried.

questions.question: Why package a PySpark project as a wheel or zip for `--py-files`?
options: To ship versioned application code and dependencies to the cluster consistently
To force Spark to use only local mode
To disable retries after task failure
To remove the need for configuration files
questions.correctAnswer: To ship versioned application code and dependencies to the cluster consistently
questions.explanation: Packaging makes deployments repeatable and avoids the fragile “copy files around manually” pattern.

questions.question: What does the principle of least privilege mean in a data platform?
options: Give each user or service only the access it genuinely needs
Grant admin rights to everyone for simplicity
Store all data in one unrestricted bucket
Disable audit logging to improve performance
questions.correctAnswer: Give each user or service only the access it genuinely needs
questions.explanation: Least privilege reduces blast radius by ensuring identities have only the permissions required for their role.

questions.question: Which control is most appropriate when analysts need a dataset but should not see raw PII values?
options: Column masking or tokenization
Broadcast joins
coalesce(1)
Disabling the metastore
questions.correctAnswer: Column masking or tokenization
questions.explanation: Masking or tokenization preserves utility while reducing exposure of sensitive information.

questions.question: Why is lineage especially valuable before making a breaking schema change?
options: It reveals which downstream jobs, dashboards, and consumers will be affected
It automatically fixes incompatible data types
It eliminates the need for access reviews
It guarantees that no pipeline will fail
questions.correctAnswer: It reveals which downstream jobs, dashboards, and consumers will be affected
questions.explanation: Lineage gives you impact awareness, which is essential before changing schemas, contracts, or access patterns.

questions.question: What does `partitionBy(...)` do in a window specification?
options: Defines the logical groups over which the window calculation resets
Physically repartitions the entire table on disk
Broadcasts each group to every executor
Caches the window output automatically
questions.correctAnswer: Defines the logical groups over which the window calculation resets
questions.explanation: partitionBy groups rows for the window function so calculations restart within each partition key.

questions.question: How does `dense_rank()` differ from `rank()` when there are ties?
options: dense_rank() does not leave gaps after ties
dense_rank() ignores ordering completely
rank() can only be used with strings
rank() always runs faster because it skips sorting
questions.correctAnswer: dense_rank() does not leave gaps after ties
questions.explanation: rank() can leave gaps after ties, while dense_rank() keeps the numbering compact.

questions.question: Which window function is the most direct way to compare a row with the previous row in the same ordered group?
options: lag()
explode()
collect_list()
broadcast()
questions.correctAnswer: lag()
questions.explanation: lag() lets you access prior rows without needing a separate self-join, which makes it a core building block for sequential analysis.

questions.question: What is one of Adaptive Query Execution’s main benefits?
options: It can adjust strategies like shuffle partition counts or join choices at runtime
It permanently removes all shuffles from a query
It moves every job into streaming mode
It stores every DataFrame in memory automatically
questions.correctAnswer: It can adjust strategies like shuffle partition counts or join choices at runtime
questions.explanation: AQE uses runtime information to improve the plan instead of relying only on static estimates.

questions.question: What does a long tail of one or two very slow tasks usually suggest?
options: Skew or straggler behavior
Perfectly balanced partitions
A successful broadcast of every table
That the driver has already cached the result
questions.correctAnswer: Skew or straggler behavior
questions.explanation: A long tail in task duration is a classic signal that one partition or one node is doing disproportionately more work.

questions.question: When is caching a DataFrame most justified?
options: When the same expensive dataset will be reused multiple times
When the dataset is used once and immediately discarded
Whenever a DataFrame contains fewer than ten columns
Only after collecting the DataFrame to the driver
questions.correctAnswer: When the same expensive dataset will be reused multiple times
questions.explanation: Caching is a tradeoff, so it pays off when recomputation would otherwise be repeated and expensive.

questions.question: In dimensional modeling, what does a fact table primarily store?
options: Measurable business events at a defined grain
Only free-form text fields
Security roles and permissions
One row per ETL job
questions.correctAnswer: Measurable business events at a defined grain
questions.explanation: Fact tables store the measurable events or transactions, while dimensions provide descriptive context.

questions.question: What is the defining property of an SCD Type 2 dimension?
options: It preserves full history by adding new rows for changes
It overwrites history in place and keeps only the latest value
It stores every field as a nested array
It can only be used in streaming pipelines
questions.correctAnswer: It preserves full history by adding new rows for changes
questions.explanation: SCD Type 2 tracks historical changes with new records, often using effective dates and a current-row indicator.

questions.question: Where do dimensional models most naturally belong in a medallion-style lakehouse?
options: Gold, because they are curated for business-facing analytics
Bronze, because they should remain raw and unmodeled
Checkpoint storage, because they are part of streaming state
Executor local disks, because they are temporary only
questions.correctAnswer: Gold, because they are curated for business-facing analytics
questions.explanation: Dimensional models are usually a gold-layer concern because they reflect curated, stable analytics structures rather than raw ingestion.

questions.question: What is the pandas API on Spark designed to provide?
options: Pandas-like syntax backed by Spark’s distributed engine
A replacement for all SQL capabilities
A way to run every job on the driver only
A storage format that replaces Delta Lake
questions.correctAnswer: Pandas-like syntax backed by Spark’s distributed engine
questions.explanation: The pandas API on Spark aims to make distributed work more approachable for teams familiar with pandas syntax.

questions.question: What is the main danger of calling `to_pandas()` on a large distributed DataFrame?
options: It collects the data to driver memory
It permanently disables Arrow
It turns the dataset into streaming state
It forces Spark to drop nested columns
questions.correctAnswer: It collects the data to driver memory
questions.explanation: to_pandas() materializes the data locally, so it is only safe when the result is genuinely small enough for the driver.

questions.question: When is the pandas API on Spark a particularly good fit?
options: When a pandas-heavy workflow has outgrown one machine but the team wants a familiar API surface
When you need the absolute minimum abstraction over JVM internals
Only when the dataset already fits easily in local RAM
Only when every transformation must be expressed in SQL
questions.correctAnswer: When a pandas-heavy workflow has outgrown one machine but the team wants a familiar API surface
questions.explanation: It is especially useful as a migration bridge for pandas users who need scale without abandoning familiar idioms immediately.

questions.question: What is the standard medallion flow for moving data toward business use?
options: Bronze → Silver → Gold
Gold → Bronze → Silver
Silver → Bronze → Archive
Gold → Checkpoint → Bronze
questions.correctAnswer: Bronze → Silver → Gold
questions.explanation: Bronze captures raw inputs, silver cleans and standardizes them, and gold serves curated business-ready outputs.

questions.question: Which Spark capability is the natural fit for near-real-time anomaly detection in a capstone data platform?
options: Structured Streaming
collect()
coalesce(1)
Static repartition hints only
questions.correctAnswer: Structured Streaming
questions.explanation: Structured Streaming is designed for continuously arriving event data and near-real-time reactions like alerting.

questions.question: Which capstone design best signals senior data-engineering judgment?
options: An end-to-end architecture with layered data, quality gates, observability, and clear operational tradeoffs
A notebook that prints sample rows and nothing else
A single script that rewrites every table in one partition nightly
A dashboard mockup without ingestion or validation logic
questions.correctAnswer: An end-to-end architecture with layered data, quality gates, observability, and clear operational tradeoffs
questions.explanation: Senior-level work is visible in system boundaries, quality controls, operations, and explicit tradeoffs, not just in a working notebook demo.

questions.question: What makes a performance-improvement story stronger in an interview?
options: Specific before-and-after metrics tied to the diagnosed root cause
Saying the pipeline became “much faster” without numbers
Avoiding any mention of tradeoffs or measurement
Focusing only on the UI theme of the dashboard
questions.correctAnswer: Specific before-and-after metrics tied to the diagnosed root cause
questions.explanation: Interviewers look for measurement discipline: what was slow, how you proved it, what changed, and what the new result was.

questions.question: What should a strong data-platform system-design answer usually cover?
options: Scale, SLAs, failure handling, data quality, and operational tradeoffs
Only syntax details from one API
Only the color palette of the UI
Only the exact Spark version number
questions.correctAnswer: Scale, SLAs, failure handling, data quality, and operational tradeoffs
questions.explanation: Good system-design answers show that you can think about reliability, scale, contracts, and operations together.

questions.question: Which portfolio project is most likely to convince a hiring manager that you understand distributed data engineering?
options: A project that explains data volume, architecture, testing, observability, and business impact with evidence
A repository with screenshots but no code or design notes
A local notebook that never touches realistic scale or failure cases
A slide deck with no runnable assets or metrics
questions.correctAnswer: A project that explains data volume, architecture, testing, observability, and business impact with evidence
questions.explanation: The strongest portfolio work demonstrates how you reasoned about distributed constraints and operational quality, not just that you produced outputs.

questions.question: In 'When Data Outgrows One Machine', which physical resource is described as 'Wall 1' — typically the first bottleneck a single-machine pipeline hits?
options: Disk read throughput — data must transit the storage interface before any transformation can run
Memory bandwidth — DRAM channels saturate well before the CPU does on tabular workloads
CPU instruction throughput — even fast cores cap at a few hundred million simple ops per second
Network bandwidth — bytes pulled from object storage cap at the NIC's line rate
questions.correctAnswer: Disk read throughput — data must transit the storage interface before any transformation can run
questions.explanation: The lesson uses the warehouse-door analogy: data must be read from disk into memory before any transformation, and modern SSDs cap at roughly 500 MB/s, so that read step is usually the first wall a growing pipeline hits.

questions.question: Lesson 02 frames distributed computing as three fundamental steps. Which option is NOT one of those three steps?
options: Compress the data before transferring it
Divide the data into pieces that can be worked on independently
Execute the work in parallel across the available executors
Combine each executor's partial result into a single final answer
questions.correctAnswer: Compress the data before transferring it
questions.explanation: The three fundamental steps are: divide the data, execute in parallel, and combine the results. Compression is an optional optimization, not a foundational step of distributed computing.

questions.question: According to 'What Spark Is', why did Spark replace Hadoop MapReduce as the dominant engine for iterative workloads?
options: It kept intermediate working data in memory between stages instead of writing it to disk every step
It introduced a distributed-computing primitive that MapReduce had no equivalent for
It replaced the JVM with a faster native runtime designed specifically for analytics
It removed the cluster manager so resources could be allocated dynamically per stage
questions.correctAnswer: It kept intermediate working data in memory between stages instead of writing it to disk every step
questions.explanation: The 'Why Spark won' callout is explicit: Spark did not invent a new concept — it removed MapReduce's mandatory disk write between stages, which is why iterative workloads got dramatically faster.

questions.question: In a Spark application, what does the driver actually hold?
options: The execution plan, metadata, and coordination logic — but not the row-level data itself
A streaming buffer of every row that passes through, used for fault recovery on retry
A mirrored copy of each executor's working set, kept in sync over the cluster network
The session's authentication credentials and the cluster manager's allocation table
questions.correctAnswer: The execution plan, metadata, and coordination logic — but not the row-level data itself
questions.explanation: Lesson 04 calls the driver 'small and smart': it holds the plan, metadata, and coordination logic only. The actual data lives on executors; the driver only sees small results that come back from actions.

questions.question: How do partitions relate to Spark's parallelism?
options: They define the unit of parallel work — at most one task per partition runs concurrently per core
They define how many rows each executor reads per second from the underlying storage
Each partition runs as its own Spark application with an independent driver and executors
Spark always allocates exactly one partition per executor regardless of dataset size
questions.correctAnswer: They define the unit of parallel work — at most one task per partition runs concurrently per core
questions.explanation: Each partition is one unit of parallel work. With 8 partitions and 8 cores, all 8 tasks run simultaneously; with more partitions than cores, cores simply pick up the next task as they finish, so partitions cap concurrency.

questions.question: How does NordGrid divide its operational territory in this course?
options: Four geographic regions: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, each with its own meters and substations
Three voltage tiers (low / medium / high) with separate billing tables per tier
Eight pricing zones derived from peak demand windows over the past year
Two operating divisions — generation and distribution — with independent meter inventories
questions.correctAnswer: Four geographic regions: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, each with its own meters and substations
questions.explanation: Lesson 06 sets up the canonical NordGrid scenario used throughout the course: four geographic regions (NORTH/SOUTH/EAST/WEST), each with its own substations, smart meters, and turbines.

questions.question: What is the primary reason the Bronze layer keeps data untransformed?
options: Reproducibility — the team can always replay any downstream step against the original raw data
Storage cost — raw text is cheaper per GB than columnar formats so Bronze stays small
Latency — skipping transformations lets dashboards read directly from Bronze with no delay
Schema flexibility — Bronze accepts any columns so the contract with sources stays loose
questions.correctAnswer: Reproducibility — the team can always replay any downstream step against the original raw data
questions.explanation: Lesson 07 makes this explicit: keeping raw data untouched in Bronze gives the team a reliable rollback point — they can always reprocess from source when a Silver or Gold output looks wrong.

questions.question: When you call `df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)` in PySpark, where does the row-level filtering actually execute?
options: Inside the JVM where Spark's engine runs — Py4J carries the Python call across to a Java method
Inside the Python interpreter on the driver, with rows streamed back from executors row by row
Inside the cluster manager (YARN or Kubernetes) which intercepts the call before scheduling
Inside the storage layer's native filter API so no rows ever reach Spark's engine at all
questions.correctAnswer: Inside the JVM where Spark's engine runs — Py4J carries the Python call across to a Java method
questions.explanation: PySpark DataFrame operations are translated through Py4J into JVM method calls; the actual row-level work runs inside Spark's JVM engine. That is why DataFrame ops in PySpark are nearly as fast as Scala — only Python UDFs round-trip back into Python.

questions.question: In Spark vocabulary, what is a *stage*?
options: A group of transformations that can run partition-locally — boundaries occur where a shuffle is required
The amount of memory each executor reserves before processing a job's first task
A separate Spark application that runs alongside the main one, sharing the cluster
The window of time between an action call and its results being collected to the driver
questions.correctAnswer: A group of transformations that can run partition-locally — boundaries occur where a shuffle is required
questions.explanation: Lesson 09 defines a stage as the largest set of transformations executable in parallel without moving data between executors. A shuffle (groupBy, join, repartition) ends one stage and begins the next.

questions.question: What is local mode useful for — and what is its main limitation?
options: Driver and executors run as threads in one JVM — fast for dev but cross-machine network behaviour can't be reproduced
It scales the same code across cluster nodes without spark-submit, only limited by per-region pricing
It substitutes a simplified Catalyst optimizer that omits join reordering for predictable behaviour
It runs only on cloud-managed JVMs and disables CPU-bound execution in favour of GPU offload
questions.correctAnswer: Driver and executors run as threads in one JVM — fast for dev but cross-machine network behaviour can't be reproduced
questions.explanation: Local mode is the standard dev/test setup: driver + executors as threads in a single process. It is fast to spin up, but shuffles in local mode skip the real network, so true cluster network effects only show up in distributed deployments.

questions.question: Before Spark 2.0, connecting to Spark required juggling SparkContext, SQLContext, and HiveContext. What did the SparkSession change?
options: It folds the pre-2.0 SparkContext / SQLContext / HiveContext into a single connection point
It removed the JVM dependency so PySpark can run against a pure-Python execution engine
It abolished the executor model — work now runs entirely on the driver in newer Spark versions
It moved cluster-resource allocation into the Spark application itself, replacing YARN/Kubernetes
questions.correctAnswer: It folds the pre-2.0 SparkContext / SQLContext / HiveContext into a single connection point
questions.explanation: Lesson 01: SparkSession is the single entry point introduced in Spark 2.0 — it consolidated SparkContext/SQLContext/HiveContext into one `spark` object that exposes data I/O, SQL, DataFrame creation, and configuration in one place.

questions.question: In `SparkSession.builder.appName('x').getOrCreate()`, what does `getOrCreate()` actually do?
options: Returns the active session if one already exists in the JVM; otherwise builds one with the configured options
Always replaces any existing session, applying the new options and discarding the old SparkContext
Raises an `AlreadyExistsException` whenever a session is already active in the same process
Builds a SparkContext but defers SparkSession construction until the first DataFrame call
questions.correctAnswer: Returns the active session if one already exists in the JVM; otherwise builds one with the configured options
questions.explanation: Lesson 02: `getOrCreate()` is deliberate — it returns the active session when one exists (typical in notebooks, where a previous cell already created it) and otherwise creates a fresh one. This makes the same code safe in both scripts and notebooks.

questions.question: Why does setting a meaningful `appName()` matter, even though it doesn't affect computation?
options: It is the human-readable label in cluster manager UIs, the Spark UI title, and log lines
Spark refuses to submit a job whose appName is missing or shorter than 8 characters
It scales the executor allocation — longer names imply higher priority in the resource queue
It changes how Catalyst names intermediate operators when generating the optimized plan
questions.correctAnswer: It is the human-readable label in cluster manager UIs, the Spark UI title, and log lines
questions.explanation: Lesson 03: the app name appears wherever an operator looks during an incident — YARN/K8s dashboards, Spark UI, alert subjects, log lines. Without a clear name, the on-call engineer paged at 3 AM has to guess which job failed.

questions.question: You want to find which step of a slow pipeline took the longest. At the junior level, which Spark UI tab is the most direct answer?
options: Stages — each stage shows duration, task count, and shuffle metrics for that part of the job
Storage — shows in-memory cache pressure and the heap composition of each executor
Environment — surfaces the active JVM and Spark configuration values resolved at startup
Executors — lists the OS user, host, and JVM PID for every executor in the application
questions.correctAnswer: Stages — each stage shows duration, task count, and shuffle metrics for that part of the job
questions.explanation: Lesson 04: at the junior level the Jobs and Stages tabs are the workhorses. Stages takes you from 'pipeline is slow' to 'this stage is the bottleneck' the fastest — durations and task counts are right there.

questions.question: How does the modern SparkSession relate to the older SparkContext?
options: SparkSession wraps SparkContext — the latter is created automatically and reachable via `spark.sparkContext`
SparkSession fully replaces SparkContext, which has been removed from PySpark since version 2.0
SparkSession is for SQL only; SparkContext is still mandatory for DataFrame and dataset operations
They live in different modules (`pyspark.sql` and `pyspark.core`) and don't share state in a single application
questions.correctAnswer: SparkSession wraps SparkContext — the latter is created automatically and reachable via `spark.sparkContext`
questions.explanation: Lesson 05: SparkSession does not replace SparkContext — it wraps it. A SparkContext is created automatically inside it; you reach it via `spark.sparkContext` when you need RDD-level work. New code should always start from `SparkSession.builder`.

questions.question: Why is hardcoding `master('local[*]')` in a script that will run in production considered a mistake?
options: It overrides whatever master spark-submit passed, so a 'cluster job' silently runs single-machine
Production drivers reject local[*] because it disables predicate pushdown and broadcast joins
It forces Spark to allocate a fixed 4 GB heap regardless of executor settings on the cluster
spark-submit refuses to start any application whose master is set programmatically in the code
questions.correctAnswer: It overrides whatever master spark-submit passed, so a 'cluster job' silently runs single-machine
questions.explanation: Lesson 06 / common-mistake callout: in production, spark-submit provides the master (e.g. `yarn`, `k8s://...`). If the code hardcodes `local[*]`, that wins — and the job that should be distributed across the cluster runs single-machine without any error.

questions.question: A developer calls `spark.conf.set('spark.executor.memory', '8g')` after the SparkSession has already started. Why doesn't it take effect?
options: Memory settings are immutable at runtime — they are allocated when executors start, so they must be set before the session is created
The property name is misspelled and Spark silently logs the typo without applying the new value
The change applies only after the next groupBy triggers a shuffle, which forces a config refresh
Memory settings can only be changed inside an active SQL session — `spark.conf.set` resets them on exit
questions.correctAnswer: Memory settings are immutable at runtime — they are allocated when executors start, so they must be set before the session is created
questions.explanation: Lesson 07: `spark.executor.memory` and `spark.driver.memory` are immutable — the cluster manager allocates that memory the moment executors start. Changing them via `spark.conf.set()` after the session is up has no effect.

questions.question: Spark configuration can be set in four places: built-in defaults, `spark-defaults.conf`, `spark-submit` flags, and `SparkSession.builder`. Which has the HIGHEST priority?
options: SparkSession.builder in code — the application's own `.config(...)` calls beat every layer below
Built-in defaults — Spark trusts its own values most because they are cluster-tested
spark-defaults.conf — the platform team's settings are non-overridable cluster policy
All four sources have equal weight; Spark merges them with the last-loaded value winning unpredictably
questions.correctAnswer: SparkSession.builder in code — the application's own `.config(...)` calls beat every layer below
questions.explanation: Lesson 08: precedence stack is defaults < spark-defaults.conf < spark-submit < SparkSession.builder. Each layer overrides the one below; code-level config is final, which is why an application can always override a platform-team default if it really needs to.

questions.question: What is the practical difference between `--deploy-mode client` and `--deploy-mode cluster` in spark-submit?
options: Client keeps the driver on the submitting host (logs stream there); cluster runs the driver on a cluster node so the job survives disconnect
Client runs only on Kubernetes; cluster mode is reserved for YARN and standalone deployments with their own resource managers
Client mode disables shuffles for performance; cluster mode runs the full join planner with shuffle support enabled
Client mode caps concurrency at one stage at a time; cluster mode parallelizes stages on the driver across all nodes
questions.correctAnswer: Client keeps the driver on the submitting host (logs stream there); cluster runs the driver on a cluster node so the job survives disconnect
questions.explanation: Lesson 09: client mode keeps the driver on the submitting host — useful for development because output streams to your terminal. Cluster mode runs the driver on a cluster node — the production choice, because the job keeps running even if the submitter disconnects.

questions.question: Why is calling `spark.stop()` (or wrapping the pipeline in try/finally) at the end of a production script important?
options: Without stopping, the cluster manager keeps the executors allocated — real memory and CPU stay reserved and unavailable to other jobs
The driver's checkpoint files only flush to disk when stop() is called, so DataFrames may be lost on exit
Catalyst can't finalize cost statistics without a stop call, and the next run plans without that history
Skipping stop() forces the next submission to use a different appName or it crashes on duplicate registration
questions.correctAnswer: Without stopping, the cluster manager keeps the executors allocated — real memory and CPU stay reserved and unavailable to other jobs
questions.explanation: Lesson 10 (and NordGrid's early-days incident): an unreleased session keeps executors allocated. A forgotten debug session held 128 GB of cluster memory overnight and starved scheduled pipelines. Production scripts wrap pipeline logic in try/finally with `spark.stop()`.

questions.question: Which set of three properties best describes a PySpark DataFrame?
options: Distributed across partitions, schema-typed, and immutable under transformations
Single-machine, dynamically typed at runtime, mutable in place for performance
GPU-resident, untyped at the column level, mutable through the `.replace()` method
Compressed on disk by default, weakly typed (everything is string), and pinned to one executor
questions.correctAnswer: Distributed across partitions, schema-typed, and immutable under transformations
questions.explanation: Lesson 01: a DataFrame is distributed (split into partitions across executors), typed (carries an explicit schema with names and data types), and immutable (any transformation produces a new DataFrame instead of mutating the old one).

questions.question: When creating a DataFrame from a Python list with `createDataFrame()`, why do production pipelines typically pass an explicit `StructType` schema instead of relying on inference?
options: It pins the column types up front so a sample drift in the source can't silently flip them mid-pipeline
It speeds up `createDataFrame` because Spark skips the row scan it normally does on Python lists
It is the only way to create a DataFrame from a list of tuples; bare lists raise a TypeError
It compresses the resulting DataFrame because the schema becomes part of the row encoding
questions.correctAnswer: It pins the column types up front so a sample drift in the source can't silently flip them mid-pipeline
questions.explanation: Lesson 02: explicit `StructType` removes guesswork. Inferred types depend on the sample values (e.g. dates passed as strings stay strings); a hard-coded schema makes the structure deterministic and self-documenting.

questions.question: What's the key schema-handling difference between reading a CSV file and reading a Parquet file in PySpark?
options: Parquet stores its schema in the file header so the reader gets correct types instantly; CSV is plain text with no type metadata
Parquet only supports primitive types (string, int, double); CSV supports the full Spark type system including structs
Parquet files require a sidecar `.schema.json` next to each data file; CSV is the actually self-describing format here
Both formats embed schemas; the only practical difference is that Parquet uses Snappy compression by default at write
questions.correctAnswer: Parquet stores its schema in the file header so the reader gets correct types instantly; CSV is plain text with no type metadata
questions.explanation: Lesson 03: CSV is plain text — every cell is a string until something else assigns a type. Parquet stores the schema as metadata in the file header, so the reader gets correct types for free, including dates and nullability.

questions.question: Why is `inferSchema=True` discouraged in production pipelines?
options: It scans the file twice, and a change in the source data can flip a column's inferred type without any error
It only works with up to ~10K rows, so it silently truncates schemas inferred from large files
It runs in driver memory, so any inference on a file larger than driver RAM raises OutOfMemory
It strips column names from the CSV header, so all columns end up named `_c0`, `_c1`, etc.
questions.correctAnswer: It scans the file twice, and a change in the source data can flip a column's inferred type without any error
questions.explanation: Lesson 04 production rule: inferSchema scans the file once just to guess types and is not deterministic — if the source format shifts (a new column appears, a numeric column starts holding text), downstream code can break in subtle ways. Production pipelines pass an explicit `StructType`.

questions.question: Which of these methods is an ACTION (triggers execution) rather than a transformation?
options: count() — returns a Python integer and triggers full execution from source to result
filter() — applies a predicate row by row and produces a new DataFrame eagerly
withColumn() — materializes the new column on every partition before returning
select() — projects columns to a new DataFrame and runs the underlying read immediately
questions.correctAnswer: count() — returns a Python integer and triggers full execution from source to result
questions.explanation: Lesson 05: `filter`, `withColumn`, and `select` are transformations — they extend the plan without running anything. `count()` is an action — it triggers a full execution from source to result. Putting `count()` inside a loop or after every step in a production pipeline runs the whole plan repeatedly.

questions.question: Why is DataFrame immutability important in a distributed system?
options: It enables safe retries — when a task fails Spark can rerun it on another executor without input mutation racing in flight
It cuts shuffle volume because Spark only ships diff-encoded changes between executors
It eliminates the need for partitions, since immutable DataFrames are inherently parallel
It compresses the DataFrame on the wire because immutability allows aggressive deduplication
questions.correctAnswer: It enables safe retries — when a task fails Spark can rerun it on another executor without input mutation racing in flight
questions.explanation: Lesson 06: in a cluster, executors crash and tasks get retried on other nodes. If data could be mutated, retries would race with in-flight writes. Immutability means every transformation produces a fresh DataFrame; the original is always safe to re-process.

questions.question: When exploring a brand-new DataFrame, which method is essentially FREE — no data is read or computed?
options: printSchema() — reads the schema metadata Spark already holds, with no executor work
show(20) — projects 20 rows to the driver but caches them, so subsequent calls are free
describe() — caches per-column statistics so the second call returns instantly with no scan
count() — uses Parquet row-group metadata to return the row count without reading any rows
questions.correctAnswer: printSchema() — reads the schema metadata Spark already holds, with no executor work
questions.explanation: Lesson 07: `printSchema()` reads the schema metadata Spark already has — no data passes through executors. `show()`, `describe()`, and `count()` all trigger a full or partial scan. The recommended exploration order is printSchema → show(5) → describe (only on a sample if the dataset is huge).

questions.question: When you write `col('daily_kw') > 0` in PySpark, what does that expression actually represent?
options: A Column object — an unevaluated expression Spark applies row by row when an action triggers
A boolean computed eagerly by reading the column from the DataFrame at that moment
A Pandas Series of booleans built from the underlying column data on the driver
An integer mask packed as a bitset, applied later via filter() to select matching rows
questions.correctAnswer: A Column object — an unevaluated expression Spark applies row by row when an action triggers
questions.explanation: Lesson 08: `col('daily_kw') > 0` does not compute anything — it builds a Column expression. The expression is later applied row-by-row when an action triggers the plan. This is why PySpark engineers think in column expressions instead of iterating row-by-row like in plain Python.

questions.question: Why is `toPandas()` dangerous on a large PySpark DataFrame?
options: It pulls every row from executors into the driver's single-machine memory; on production-scale data the driver typically OOMs
It permanently converts the DataFrame, so subsequent Spark operations against it raise an error
Pandas isn't bundled with PySpark, so toPandas() throws ImportError unless you pip install it first
It silently downsamples the DataFrame to 10K rows and discards the rest without warning
questions.correctAnswer: It pulls every row from executors into the driver's single-machine memory; on production-scale data the driver typically OOMs
questions.explanation: Lesson 09: `toPandas()` collects ALL data into a single Pandas DataFrame in driver memory. It's fine for small results (a chart, a 1000-row sample) but catastrophic on production-scale DataFrames — the driver isn't sized for full-dataset materialization.

questions.question: Every PySpark pipeline follows the same three-phase pattern. What are the three phases, in order?
options: Read the source, apply a chain of lazy transformations, trigger execution with an action
Configure the session, schedule each step on the driver, log the result to the catalog
Compile to bytecode, plan the execution graph, optimize for the target compression codec
Allocate executors, distribute partitions evenly, collect every result back to the driver
questions.correctAnswer: Read the source, apply a chain of lazy transformations, trigger execution with an action
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: Read loads data into a DataFrame, Transform applies a chain of lazy operations (filter, withColumn, join), Act calls a terminating action (write, count, show) that triggers the optimized plan to run end-to-end.

questions.question: Why is `select()` both a shaping tool and a performance tool when reading from Parquet?
options: Catalyst pushes the column list into the Parquet reader so only the chosen columns are read from disk
It applies dictionary encoding to the remaining columns at write time to shrink them on disk
It forces single-executor execution, which avoids the network cost of distributing partitions
It bypasses Catalyst entirely, so no optimization overhead is added on top of the read
questions.correctAnswer: Catalyst pushes the column list into the Parquet reader so only the chosen columns are read from disk
questions.explanation: Lesson 01: Parquet stores data column-by-column, so when Catalyst sees a `select(...)` it pushes that list down to the reader and only the chosen columns are physically read. Selecting fewer columns directly cuts I/O — it isn't just code clarity.

questions.question: When are `select('daily_kw')` and `select(col('daily_kw'))` NOT interchangeable?
options: When you need an expression — arithmetic, comparison, function call — a bare string is just a name and can't carry one
When the column has a special character in its name; col() escapes it but a string passes through unchanged
When the schema is dynamic; only col() resolves at runtime, strings resolve at compile time
When the operation runs on Parquet; col() pushes filters down but a string does not
questions.correctAnswer: When you need an expression — arithmetic, comparison, function call — a bare string is just a name and can't carry one
questions.explanation: Lesson 02: a string is a column reference; a `col()` expression can be composed (`col('daily_kw') * 1000`, `col('daily_kw') > 0`). For plain selection both are fine and equivalent; for any expression you need the Column object.

questions.question: What's a meaningful behavioral difference between `select()` and `drop()` for columns that don't exist?
options: select() raises AnalysisException; drop() silently ignores names that aren't there
Both raise; the only difference is the wording of the error message
Both silently ignore; missing columns never produce errors regardless of which method is used
drop() raises AnalysisException; select() returns an empty DataFrame with no columns
questions.correctAnswer: select() raises AnalysisException; drop() silently ignores names that aren't there
questions.explanation: Lesson 03: that asymmetry matters in practice. `drop('legacy_col')` is safe to leave in code even when the column may not appear in some inputs; `select('legacy_col')` will fail. NordGrid's pattern: `drop()` for cleanup mid-pipeline, `select()` to pin the final schema at the end.

questions.question: What's the relationship between PySpark's `filter()` and `where()`?
options: Exact aliases — same implementation, identical execution plan, choice is style only
where() runs the SQL parser; filter() takes only Column objects, so the underlying paths differ
filter() is lazy; where() executes immediately and returns a Python list of matching rows
filter() works on RDDs; where() works on DataFrames — they're rarely interchangeable
questions.correctAnswer: Exact aliases — same implementation, identical execution plan, choice is style only
questions.explanation: Lesson 04: `filter()` and `where()` are aliases of the same method. Some teams pick `filter()` for the Pythonic feel, others `where()` for SQL familiarity — Catalyst sees them identically.

questions.question: Why must you parenthesise each comparison when combining filter conditions with `&` or `|` in PySpark?
options: Python's `&` and `|` bind tighter than `>` or `==`, so without parens the expression parses against the operands instead of the boolean results
PySpark requires parens because the JVM-side parser treats `&` as a string-concatenation operator otherwise
They're only required in cluster mode; in local mode Spark relaxes operator precedence rules
They're a stylistic recommendation only — Spark resolves the precedence correctly without them
questions.correctAnswer: Python's `&` and `|` bind tighter than `>` or `==`, so without parens the expression parses against the operands instead of the boolean results
questions.explanation: Lesson 05: `&` and `|` are bitwise operators in Python and bind TIGHTER than comparisons. The fix is `(col('a') > 0) & (col('b') == 'X')` — wrap each comparison so the boolean operators apply to whole booleans, not to their operands.

questions.question: Why does `col('daily_kw') == None` not work for finding null values, and what's the correct way?
options: Three-valued logic — comparing anything to null yields null, not true, so filter() drops the rows; use isNull() instead
The Python `None` doesn't translate to SQL NULL; PySpark serializes it as the literal string 'None'
`==` is overridden for Column objects to mean 'structural equality,' not value comparison
It works fine; the lesson refers to an older PySpark behaviour that was fixed in 2.4
questions.correctAnswer: Three-valued logic — comparing anything to null yields null, not true, so filter() drops the rows; use isNull() instead
questions.explanation: Lesson 06: in three-valued logic, `null == null` is null, not true. `filter()` only keeps rows where the condition is truly true, so any null comparison silently discards the rows you wanted. `isNull()` / `isNotNull()` are the correct primitives.

questions.question: How does the execution plan differ between `df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)` and `df.filter('daily_kw > 0')`?
options: Identical — Spark parses the SQL string into the same logical plan as the Column expression
The Column form skips parsing so it's measurably faster on every query
The string form runs through Hive's parser, which adds a few hundred microseconds per call
They differ only in null handling — SQL strings treat null as false, Column expressions preserve it
questions.correctAnswer: Identical — Spark parses the SQL string into the same logical plan as the Column expression
questions.explanation: Lesson 07: the SQL string is parsed into a Column expression before optimization, so Catalyst sees the same logical plan either way. Choose by readability — Column expressions for IDE-aware refactoring, SQL strings when the team thinks in SQL.

questions.question: What's the cleanest way to rename `kwh_reading` to `daily_kw` while ALSO trimming the DataFrame down to just four columns?
options: select() with `.alias('daily_kw')` on the column — selection and rename happen in one pass with no extra columns surviving
withColumnRenamed('kwh_reading', 'daily_kw') — it both renames and drops other columns automatically
toDF('daily_kw') — assigns the new name to the only surviving column based on positional order
A chain of withColumn() calls — one to rename, one to drop, one to reorder — for explicit intent
questions.correctAnswer: select() with `.alias('daily_kw')` on the column — selection and rename happen in one pass with no extra columns surviving
questions.explanation: Lesson 08: `select(... col('kwh_reading').alias('daily_kw') ...)` shapes and renames in one step. `withColumnRenamed` only renames — it would leave every other column intact, so you'd still need `select()` to trim.

questions.question: When `dropDuplicates(['meter_id', 'date'])` finds multiple rows with the same `(meter_id, date)`, which row does it keep?
options: Arbitrary — depends on partition order and execution timing; for deterministic results sort or use a window function first
Always the row with the latest ingestion_timestamp value within the duplicate group
Always the first row in lexicographic order across the deduplication key columns
Always the row whose hash falls into the lowest-numbered partition for the chosen seed
questions.correctAnswer: Arbitrary — depends on partition order and execution timing; for deterministic results sort or use a window function first
questions.explanation: Lesson 09 warning: `dropDuplicates` is non-deterministic on the kept row. If you need 'latest reading wins' semantics, sort by ingestion_timestamp first or use a window function with `row_number() over (partition by … order by …)` and keep `row_number = 1`.

questions.question: Eight transformations chained together — does that mean Spark scans the dataset eight times?
options: One pass — Spark records the chain into a plan and Catalyst executes it as one optimized scan
Eight passes — each transformation reads the source independently and writes to disk in between
Two passes — the first builds statistics, the second runs the full computation with those stats
One pass per filter, but withColumn and select each trigger their own scan in addition
questions.correctAnswer: One pass — Spark records the chain into a plan and Catalyst executes it as one optimized scan
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: chaining is free. Spark builds the entire chain as a logical plan, lets Catalyst combine and reorder operations (filter pushdown, column pruning, predicate combining), and only physically scans the data when an action like `write` runs.

questions.question: What happens when you call `withColumn('region', upper(col('region')))` on a DataFrame that already has a `region` column?
options: It REPLACES the column's values with the new expression, keeping the column in its original position
It raises an exception because the column name is already taken in the schema
It creates a second column named `region_1` and leaves the original untouched
It drops the original column and appends a new one named `region` at the end of the schema
questions.correctAnswer: It REPLACES the column's values with the new expression, keeping the column in its original position
questions.explanation: Lesson 01: passing an existing column name to `withColumn()` replaces that column's values in place. The column keeps its original position — useful for type casts, normalization, and cleanup without restructuring the schema.

questions.question: Which renaming technique applies new names POSITIONALLY (first name → first column, regardless of current name)?
options: toDF('a', 'b', 'c', ...) — assigns the new names by position regardless of the existing column names
withColumnRenamed('old', 'new') — Spark resolves the positional mapping from the schema metadata
alias() inside select() — applies the new names positionally to whatever you selected
A `for` loop calling withColumn(new, col(old)) — Spark internally maps the loop to a positional rename
questions.correctAnswer: toDF('a', 'b', 'c', ...) — assigns the new names by position regardless of the existing column names
questions.explanation: Lesson 02: `toDF()` accepts a list of names and assigns them positionally — first name to first column, etc. It doesn't look at the existing names at all. Useful for renaming all columns at once when you trust the order; risky if column order ever changes.

questions.question: What happens if you `cast('double')` a string column where some values are 'N/A' instead of numbers?
options: Spark silently produces null for the bad rows — the rest of the column casts fine, no error raised
Spark raises an analysis exception and aborts the whole job before any data is read
Spark coerces the bad value to its type's zero (0.0 for double, 0 for int, '' for string)
Spark writes the bad rows to a quarantine path under the source directory and logs a warning
questions.correctAnswer: Spark silently produces null for the bad rows — the rest of the column casts fine, no error raised
questions.explanation: Lesson 03 warning: cast failures don't throw — they produce null. The defensive pattern is to count nulls before and after the cast: a sudden jump in nulls means a data quality issue you'd otherwise miss until a downstream report looked wrong.

questions.question: Why are PySpark's built-in string functions (upper, trim, regexp_replace, etc.) much faster than wrapping equivalent Python logic in a UDF?
options: Built-ins execute in the JVM at native speed; UDFs serialize each row to Python and back, paying that cost twice per row
They run on dedicated string-processing GPU kernels, which Spark added in version 3.0 for analytics workloads
They cache the input column in driver memory the first time it's referenced, so subsequent calls are essentially free
They are JIT-compiled into native code by the OS each time the executor starts, similar to Numba's approach
questions.correctAnswer: Built-ins execute in the JVM at native speed; UDFs serialize each row to Python and back, paying that cost twice per row
questions.explanation: Lesson 04: built-in string functions compile to JVM bytecode and process partitions in-place. UDFs cross the JVM↔Python boundary for every row, paying serialization cost twice per row. Always prefer built-ins.

questions.question: What's NordGrid's recommended way to handle dates throughout a pipeline?
options: Keep dates as DateType / TimestampType throughout; convert to formatted strings only at the final output step
Convert dates to ISO strings on read; PySpark's date functions all work on strings without any conversion needed
Store every date as a Unix timestamp (long); it's the most compact representation and skips the parser overhead
Encode dates as YYYYMMDD integers; arithmetic and equality comparisons run faster on int than on DateType
questions.correctAnswer: Keep dates as DateType / TimestampType throughout; convert to formatted strings only at the final output step
questions.explanation: Lesson 05: working with dates as proper temporal types lets you use date arithmetic, comparisons, and date-part extraction efficiently. Format-as-string only at the output boundary so downstream consumers get the format they need.

questions.question: If `daily_kw` is null in a row, what is the value of `col('daily_kw') * 0.12 + 5`?
options: Null — any arithmetic involving a null operand propagates null through the entire expression
5 — the literal 5 is added even when daily_kw is null because constants override null
0 — Spark's arithmetic treats null as a missing value identical to zero for backwards compatibility
An exception — Spark raises NullPointerException whenever a null reaches an arithmetic expression
questions.correctAnswer: Null — any arithmetic involving a null operand propagates null through the entire expression
questions.explanation: Lesson 06: null propagates through arithmetic. `null * 0.12` is null, `null + 5` is null. Use `coalesce()` or `when(col('x').isNotNull(), ...).otherwise(...)` to substitute a default before doing math.

questions.question: What SQL construct does `when(cond1, val1).when(cond2, val2).otherwise(default)` correspond to?
options: CASE WHEN cond1 THEN val1 WHEN cond2 THEN val2 ELSE default END
IF cond THEN val ELSE val END — Spark SQL only supports the IF form, not CASE WHEN
A series of UNION ALL queries, one per branch, joined by row position
A WHERE clause with multiple conditions OR'd together against the same expression
questions.correctAnswer: CASE WHEN cond1 THEN val1 WHEN cond2 THEN val2 ELSE default END
questions.explanation: Lesson 07: `when().when().otherwise()` is the PySpark equivalent of SQL's CASE WHEN. Conditions are evaluated top-down; the first match wins. Without `otherwise()`, unmatched rows get null.

questions.question: Why might NordGrid materialize a boolean `is_valid` column on the Silver table instead of repeating the filter condition wherever it's needed?
options: The business rule lives in one column — every downstream query filters on the flag, so a rule change updates one place
Boolean columns compress better than the original numeric columns, shrinking the table on disk significantly
Spark requires a boolean flag column on every Silver table for partition pruning to work correctly at read time
Filters run faster against a precomputed boolean than against the original predicate expression every time
questions.correctAnswer: The business rule lives in one column — every downstream query filters on the flag, so a rule change updates one place
questions.explanation: Lesson 08: a flag column turns business logic into a label. When the rule changes (e.g. tighter null-handling), you update the flag definition once instead of finding every duplicated filter expression scattered across reports.

questions.question: What does `expr('CASE WHEN daily_kw < 500 THEN "low" ELSE "high" END')` return?
options: A Column object you can use anywhere a Column is accepted — withColumn, select, filter, and so on
A SQL view automatically registered in the session catalog under the name `expr_<n>`
A Python string ready to be passed into spark.sql() for execution
A SQLExpression object that's separate from Column and only works inside agg() calls
questions.correctAnswer: A Column object you can use anywhere a Column is accepted — withColumn, select, filter, and so on
questions.explanation: Lesson 09: `expr()` parses a SQL string and returns a regular Column object. The optimized plan is identical to the equivalent `when().otherwise()` chain — choose by readability.

questions.question: A 50-line Bronze-to-Silver pipeline chains 8 transformations. How many physical scans of the source CSV does Spark perform when the final write triggers?
options: Just one — Catalyst sees the whole chain, optimizes it, and reads the source data once
Eight — every transformation triggers its own pass to materialize intermediate results to disk
Two — one pass to compute statistics, one pass to apply transformations using those stats
Sixteen — Spark reads twice per transformation (once for input, once for output verification)
questions.correctAnswer: Just one — Catalyst sees the whole chain, optimizes it, and reads the source data once
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: lazy evaluation lets Catalyst combine all transformations into one plan. The physical execution does column pruning, predicate pushdown, and filter combining, then makes a single pass over the source.

questions.question: What's the defining characteristic that distinguishes aggregation from row-level operations like filter or withColumn?
options: Aggregation REDUCES the number of rows — many rows become few rows (or one row)
Aggregation runs strictly faster than row-level operations because it processes less data per row
Aggregation always increases the number of columns by inserting one summary column per metric
Aggregation is restricted to Parquet files because it depends on column-oriented storage
questions.correctAnswer: Aggregation REDUCES the number of rows — many rows become few rows (or one row)
questions.explanation: Lesson 01: filter/select/withColumn keep one row per input row. Aggregation collapses many rows into summaries — 500,000 readings into 4 regional totals. That row reduction is the bridge from raw data to information.

questions.question: Why is it standard to import PySpark's aggregation functions with aliases (e.g. `from pyspark.sql.functions import sum as spark_sum`)?
options: Python has built-in functions called sum, min, max — without an alias the import shadows them and breaks plain Python code in the same file
The aliased names execute faster because Spark caches the aliased reference at import time
Spark requires aliases for any function whose name matches a Python keyword, including sum/min/max
It's a stylistic preference for SQL-heavy teams; the names work identically without aliases
questions.correctAnswer: Python has built-in functions called sum, min, max — without an alias the import shadows them and breaks plain Python code in the same file
questions.explanation: Lesson 02 warning: `from pyspark.sql.functions import sum` overrides Python's built-in `sum`. Aliasing as `spark_sum` keeps both available — important when you need plain Python `sum(...)` next to PySpark `spark_sum(...)`.

questions.question: Is `groupBy('region').agg(sum('daily_kw'))` an action or a transformation?
options: A transformation — it builds the plan but doesn't execute until an action like show, count, or write triggers it
An action — it executes immediately and returns a Python dict mapping each group key to its aggregate
Neither — groupBy is a metadata-only operation that registers the grouping with the catalog
Both — groupBy is lazy but agg is eager, so the chain runs partway as soon as agg is called
questions.correctAnswer: A transformation — it builds the plan but doesn't execute until an action like show, count, or write triggers it
questions.explanation: Lesson 03: groupBy + agg is lazy. It looks like 'do the work,' but no rows are scanned until an action fires. This is why you can safely chain groupBy with further filters and selects without paying for intermediate computation.

questions.question: Why should you alias every aggregation expression inside `agg()`?
options: Without alias() the output column is named `sum(daily_kw)` — parentheses break SQL queries and downstream selects
alias() is required for groupBy to deduplicate rows, otherwise duplicates leak into the result
Aliasing improves performance by letting Catalyst skip the operator-name resolution step
Spark refuses to materialize a DataFrame whose column names contain function-application syntax
questions.correctAnswer: Without alias() the output column is named `sum(daily_kw)` — parentheses break SQL queries and downstream selects
questions.explanation: Lesson 04: an unnamed aggregation gets a default column name with parentheses. That name is awkward to reference (`col('sum(daily_kw)')`) and unfriendly to SQL. Always alias for clean downstream contracts.

questions.question: Where should you filter out unwanted rows when you can do it either before or after a groupBy?
options: Before — pre-filtering reduces the rows that enter the (shuffle-bound) aggregation
After — Catalyst is more aggressive on aggregated results so the same filter runs faster there
It makes no difference because Spark always pushes filters above and below the aggregation
Before is illegal — filter() must come after groupBy() because groupBy locks the schema first
questions.correctAnswer: Before — pre-filtering reduces the rows that enter the (shuffle-bound) aggregation
questions.explanation: Lesson 05: filter-before-groupBy is the WHERE pattern, filter-after is HAVING. Catalyst pushes simple filters down on its own, but expressing your intent up front guarantees the shuffle processes only the rows you want.

questions.question: Why is high cardinality a problem for the column you pivot on?
options: Each unique value becomes its own output column — high cardinality produces an unusably wide DataFrame that's slow to write
Pivot can only handle string-typed columns; numeric pivot keys require an explicit `castAs` to string first to work
High-cardinality pivots silently truncate to the first 64 distinct values and discard the rest without a warning
Spark forbids pivot on columns flagged as `nullable=true`, which most high-cardinality columns happen to be by default
questions.correctAnswer: Each unique value becomes its own output column — high cardinality produces an unusably wide DataFrame that's slow to write
questions.explanation: Lesson 06 warning: pivot turns row values into column headers. Region (4 values) is a great pivot column. Meter ID (500K values) would create 500K columns — broken in every direction. Pre-supply the value list when you know it.

questions.question: Why does `groupBy().agg()` always trigger a shuffle, while `filter()` and `withColumn()` do not?
options: Aggregation is a wide transformation — rows with the same key may live on different partitions, so Spark must redistribute them onto the same executor first
Aggregation accumulates values into driver-side buffers, and the network round-trip to the driver is what is called the shuffle
Catalyst always inserts a shuffle step before any transformation it does not have column-level statistics available for
Shuffle is a configuration option enabled by default for groupBy; turning it off keeps work partition-local but loses results
questions.correctAnswer: Aggregation is a wide transformation — rows with the same key may live on different partitions, so Spark must redistribute them onto the same executor first
