questions.correctAnswer: Type 1 keeps only current state; Type 2 keeps every version with effective_from / effective_to columns
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: the choice is whether you need history. Type 1 just overwrites — losing the old value. Type 2 preserves it with valid-time columns, enabling "what was true on date X" queries.

questions.question: NordGrid's three-stage pipeline uses "completion markers" to enable what?
options: Resuming after failure — re-runs skip stages whose marker shows SUCCESS; only the failed stage and downstream run
Faster initial runs — the marker file is smaller than the output, so the first read is quick on cold runs
Schema enforcement — the marker contains the expected schema; mismatches fail before write to disk
Cross-cluster deployment — the marker gets replicated to the DR cluster automatically by the orchestrator
questions.correctAnswer: Resuming after failure — re-runs skip stages whose marker shows SUCCESS; only the failed stage and downstream run
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: completion markers turn a multi-stage pipeline into a resumable one. After a failure on stage 3, the re-run skips stages 1-2 (already SUCCESS) and starts at stage 3. Saves time on every retry.

questions.question: NordGrid's rule for what belongs in YAML CONFIG vs in CODE is:
options: Anything that might differ across environments or change without a code change goes in YAML
Anything related to data — paths, schemas, tables — goes in YAML; logic always stays in code
Any string longer than 50 characters goes in YAML to keep code readable on screen
Anything that might be wrong stays in code, where review processes catch errors before merge
questions.correctAnswer: Anything that might differ across environments or change without a code change goes in YAML
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: the test is "would changing this require a code change?". If yes (logic), it stays in code. If no (paths, thresholds, partition counts), it belongs in YAML.

questions.question: Quality gates fire BEFORE the write to Silver/Gold rather than after. Why?
options: Bad data shouldn't reach downstream consumers — fail fast and revert to the last known good state
After-the-write gates can't read the file Spark just wrote because of caching delays in storage
Pre-write gates run inside the same query plan, making them 10× faster than post-write gates
Quality gate libraries only support pre-write hooks; post-write hooks were deprecated in Spark 3.x
questions.correctAnswer: Bad data shouldn't reach downstream consumers — fail fast and revert to the last known good state
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: pre-write gates are circuit breakers. If the data fails quality, you skip the write and the downstream consumers continue using yesterday's good data. Post-write gates discover the bad data after others have already started using it.

questions.question: Pipeline metadata columns (`_pipeline_version`, `_ingestion_timestamp`, `_source_file`) help with:
options: Lineage queries — answering "which source file produced this row?" without manual debugging
Performance — Catalyst uses them to skip rows from old pipeline versions automatically at read time
Schema evolution — they let Delta detect when a column has been renamed across pipeline versions
Compression — they enable Parquet to dictionary-encode the rest of the row more efficiently
questions.correctAnswer: Lineage queries — answering "which source file produced this row?" without manual debugging
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: lineage. When a stakeholder questions a number, the metadata columns let you answer "this row came from this source file, processed by this pipeline version, at this time" with a SQL query.

questions.question: NordGrid's pipeline architecture is "the product of 18 months of production operation." How should new pipelines adopt it?
options: Each pattern was added in response to a specific incident — start with idempotency, layer in others as pain demands
Replicate the entire stack on day one of any new pipeline — partial adoption is unsafe in production
Use as a reference but don't apply directly — every team needs its own 18-month iteration to mature
The patterns are mandatory for Apache Spark certification; certification requires implementing all of them
questions.correctAnswer: Each pattern was added in response to a specific incident — start with idempotency, layer in others as pain demands
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: don't copy-paste the whole architecture on day one. Each pattern was added because something hurt. Start with idempotency (most pipelines need it) and add the rest as the team experiences the pain.

questions.question: The "filter typo incident" (`col('daily_kw') > 0` → `>= 0`) demonstrates what failure mode?
options: Silent data corruption — the pipeline runs successfully, schema matches, but the numbers are wrong
A schema mismatch — the new operator produced a different output type that downstream readers rejected
Performance regression — the new condition was 10× slower to evaluate against the input column
A null pointer exception — Python's > operator raised when it encountered nulls in the column
questions.correctAnswer: Silent data corruption — the pipeline runs successfully, schema matches, but the numbers are wrong
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: silent corruption is the worst class of bug — no error, no schema mismatch, just wrong answers. Tests catch it because they assert specific output values, not just "ran successfully".

questions.question: A unit test for a PySpark transformation follows what pattern?
options: Create small input DF with known values → apply the transformation → assert output matches expected
Read a slice from the production table → apply the transformation → write to a temporary path
Mock the SparkSession entirely → invoke the function → check no real Spark calls happened during
Generate a random 10,000-row DF → apply the transformation → check the output is non-empty
questions.correctAnswer: Create small input DF with known values → apply the transformation → assert output matches expected
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: arrange-act-assert with controlled inputs. Each row in the input DF tests a specific edge case. The expected output is hand-calculated. Random data and production reads aren't reliable test inputs.

questions.question: Why share a single SparkSession across all tests via a session-scoped pytest fixture?
options: A SparkSession takes 5–15 seconds to start; sharing keeps a 30-test suite under a minute instead of several
Each test must run in the same session for Catalyst's plan cache to function correctly across files
Shared sessions prevent resource leaks; per-test sessions accumulate and exhaust the cluster pool
It is required by pyspark.testing — the assertion library only works with shared session instances
questions.correctAnswer: A SparkSession takes 5–15 seconds to start; sharing keeps a 30-test suite under a minute instead of several
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: SparkSession startup is the dominant test-suite cost. A session-scoped fixture starts it once for all tests, turning a 5-minute suite into a 30-second one.

questions.question: NordGrid's test data convention uses INLINE creation for unit tests because:
options: Each row is visible in the test file with a documented purpose — the test self-documents what it covers
Inline data is faster to load than fixture files, which require disk I/O even for small data sets
pytest can only discover tests whose data is defined in-line in the test function definition
Fixture files require a Hive metastore registration, which is not available in CI environments
questions.correctAnswer: Each row is visible in the test file with a documented purpose — the test self-documents what it covers
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: inline data turns the test into a complete document. A reader sees the inputs, the transformation, and the assertions in one place. Fixture files separate inputs from logic and obscure intent.

questions.question: An integration test for a pipeline does WHAT that a unit test does not?
options: Runs the full read→transform→write chain on a fixture, verifying integration of all stages end-to-end
Tests against a copy of production data instead of the synthetic test inputs used by unit tests
Verifies that the SparkSession is correctly torn down between test runs in the suite
Checks the underlying Parquet's compression ratio is within an acceptable range for output
questions.correctAnswer: Runs the full read→transform→write chain on a fixture, verifying integration of all stages end-to-end
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: integration tests catch bugs in stage boundaries — schema drift between transform and write, file format issues at the read step. Unit tests can't see those because they exercise one function at a time.

questions.question: A "golden schema" file stored alongside pipeline code serves what purpose?
options: CI runs the code on test data and asserts the output schema matches the file — schema regressions caught pre-merge
Spark loads it at runtime as the canonical schema, ignoring whatever the data files actually contain
It is a backup of the production schema for disaster recovery and incident-response scenarios
Delta Lake reads it as the merge baseline when the source has a different schema than expected
questions.correctAnswer: CI runs the code on test data and asserts the output schema matches the file — schema regressions caught pre-merge
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: golden schema = canonical contract. CI compares the test run's output schema to the file. Any unintended change requires the engineer to either fix the bug or update the golden file (and document why).

questions.question: Quality gate tests should verify both directions. What does "both directions" mean?
options: Test that the gate FAILS on bad data AND PASSES on good data — false negatives and false positives both ruled out
Test the gate at both pipeline ingress and egress, since failures look different at each boundary
Run the test on both Spark 3.0 and 3.5 to ensure cross-version compatibility for the gate logic
Assert both row count and schema in every test, never just one of the two assertions
questions.correctAnswer: Test that the gate FAILS on bad data AND PASSES on good data — false negatives and false positives both ruled out
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: a gate is only useful if it catches problems AND doesn't cry wolf. Tests must verify both: bad data → fails (catches), good data → passes (no false positives blocking valid pipelines).

questions.question: NordGrid's "edge case library" with factory functions like `make_empty_readings(spark)` exists because:
options: Edge cases get reused across many tests; centralizing them avoids divergent ad-hoc DataFrames per test
Factory functions are required by pytest's parametrize decorator for inline data definitions
Spark caches DataFrames built by the same factory call, so reuse improves test execution speed
Factory functions can be pickled and shipped to executors; ad-hoc DataFrames cannot be serialized
questions.correctAnswer: Edge cases get reused across many tests; centralizing them avoids divergent ad-hoc DataFrames per test
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: every test reaches for the same edge cases (empty, all-null, single row). A library means "one definition of empty readings" across tests. Without it, each test cooks its own slightly-different version.

questions.question: NordGrid's CI runs UNIT tests on every PR but INTEGRATION tests only on merge. Why?
options: Unit tests are fast (~15 seconds); integration tests are slow (minutes) — the speed/coverage trade-off
Unit tests run on isolated runners; integration tests need the production cluster which PRs cannot access
Integration tests have flaky network dependencies that would block too many PRs from merging
Spark's licensing forbids running integration tests outside the main branch in any environment
questions.correctAnswer: Unit tests are fast (~15 seconds); integration tests are slow (minutes) — the speed/coverage trade-off
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: PR feedback should be fast (under a minute total). Integration tests are slower so they run after merge, blocking deployment if they fail but not blocking the merge itself.

questions.question: NordGrid measured testing's ROI in year one. What did they find?
options: Caught 4 bugs that would have reached production with combined estimated impact larger than the test investment
Reduced cluster cost by 30% via test-suite-driven shuffle partition tuning across all production pipelines
Eliminated all production incidents; the team had zero pages from data quality issues across the year
Compressed deployment time from days to hours by replacing manual review with automated tests entirely
questions.correctAnswer: Caught 4 bugs that would have reached production with combined estimated impact larger than the test investment
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: tests are insurance — pay-now to avoid pay-later. A handful of caught bugs paid for the entire test infrastructure investment. The actual ROI is even higher when you count incidents that were never logged because tests caught them.

questions.question: NordGrid's rule "no notebook is scheduled as a production job" is motivated by:
options: Notebooks lack structure, parameterization, error handling, version control — they break in ways unit tests can't catch
Notebooks cannot authenticate to the cluster; only `.py` scripts authenticate via service account tokens
Notebooks have a 100 MB cell-size limit that production data quickly exceeds in real workloads
Spark cannot trigger notebook runs from spark-submit; only from the Jupyter web UI directly
questions.correctAnswer: Notebooks lack structure, parameterization, error handling, version control — they break in ways unit tests can't catch
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: notebooks are for exploration, not production. Production needs reproducibility (version control), parameterization (date arguments), error handling (try/finally), and structure (modules) — none of which notebooks provide cleanly.

questions.question: NordGrid generates spark-submit commands from YAML, never typed by hand. Why?
options: Resource flags drift across environments and team members; YAML is the single source of truth that CI validates
Manually typed commands are limited to 4096 characters; complex pipelines exceed this length consistently
Spark requires generated commands; manually-typed commands are only allowed in development environments
Generated commands include digital signatures that the cluster manager validates at submit time for security
questions.correctAnswer: Resource flags drift across environments and team members; YAML is the single source of truth that CI validates
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: hand-typed commands diverge between dev/staging/prod and across team members. YAML centralizes the truth — if executor.memory is in YAML, every environment uses the same value (or an explicit override).

questions.question: NordGrid's Airflow integration uses `BashOperator` calling a YAML wrapper, not `SparkSubmitOperator` directly. Why?
options: The wrapper is the single integration point — config validation, dependency assembly, and version pinning live there
BashOperator is faster than SparkSubmitOperator for small pipelines under one minute of execution time
SparkSubmitOperator was deprecated in Airflow 2.5; BashOperator is the recommended replacement going forward
BashOperator allows running multiple pipelines in parallel; SparkSubmitOperator serializes submissions per DAG
questions.correctAnswer: The wrapper is the single integration point — config validation, dependency assembly, and version pinning live there
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: every pipeline goes through the same wrapper. The wrapper does config validation, dependency setup, and version pinning. Replacing it across many DAGs is one change in one place.

questions.question: What's the CRITICAL principle for environment management?
options: Production paths must NEVER appear in dev configuration; one wrong path can corrupt production data
All three environments (dev, staging, prod) should share the same config file with feature flags toggled
Use environment variables exclusively, never YAML — env vars are encrypted by the orchestrator at rest
Always run dev against a snapshot of prod data; relying on synthetic test data hides production bugs
questions.correctAnswer: Production paths must NEVER appear in dev configuration; one wrong path can corrupt production data
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: separation is non-negotiable. A dev run that accidentally references a prod path can overwrite production data. The config files must be entirely separate, with environment chosen explicitly at submit time.

questions.question: `--py-files` distributes Python files to executors. What's the equivalent for Java/Scala dependencies?
options: `--packages` with Maven coordinates — Spark downloads from Maven Central at submit time and ships them
`--jvm-jars` — an explicit list of pre-installed cluster-side JAR paths the driver and executors can find
`--driver-class-path` — the only mechanism, but limited to the driver's classpath (not executors)
`--scala-deps` — accepts Scala source files which Spark compiles inline before launching executors
questions.correctAnswer: `--packages` with Maven coordinates — Spark downloads from Maven Central at submit time and ships them
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: --packages is the canonical mechanism. `delta-spark`, `kafka-clients`, etc. are pulled by Maven coordinates and shipped to every executor automatically.

questions.question: NordGrid's CI rule "main is always deployable" means operationally:
options: Every merge to main passes lint, unit tests, integration tests, config validation, and schema checks before merge
The main branch is replicated to the production cluster within 60 seconds of any merge automatically
Engineers can deploy main without approval at any time of day, including weekends and holidays
The main branch has commit signing enabled; unsigned commits are automatically rejected by the repo
questions.correctAnswer: Every merge to main passes lint, unit tests, integration tests, config validation, and schema checks before merge
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: "deployable" means the gate of pre-merge checks has cleared everything that would block a deploy. The merge itself doesn't deploy; the merge means deploy is now safe whenever requested.

questions.question: NordGrid's CI catches ~5% of PRs with operational issues that unit tests miss. A typical example?
options: A code change that accidentally adds a UDF, breaking pushdown — unit tests pass but operational performance regresses
A test failure on Spark 3.0 that does not affect Spark 3.5 — version-skew issues across environments
A dependency upgrade that pyproject.toml does not pin — only CI's resolver catches the silent drift
A typo in a Markdown file — CI's link checker catches it before the merge to the main branch
questions.correctAnswer: A code change that accidentally adds a UDF, breaking pushdown — unit tests pass but operational performance regresses
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: unit tests check correctness on small data. CI plan-verification checks operational quality — pushdown still works, broadcast still happens, no new shuffles. These are invisible to unit tests but show up in production.

questions.question: For a "transformation logic change with >80% test coverage", which deployment strategy does NordGrid use?
options: Blue-green — run new code alongside old, write to parallel paths, compare results before cutover
Direct deployment — replace immediately; the test coverage justifies the confidence in the change
Canary — route 10% of traffic through the new code in production while the rest stays on old
No deployment — ship as a feature flag and let stakeholders enable it manually when ready
questions.correctAnswer: Blue-green — run new code alongside old, write to parallel paths, compare results before cutover
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: NordGrid's risk matrix maps change type to strategy. Logic changes are higher risk than config changes — blue-green lets the team observe the new code's output for a day before promoting it.

questions.question: NordGrid's `daily_kw` cast incident (`cast('double')` → `cast('float')`) revealed what gap?
options: Tests used approximate equality; the precision loss only mattered at production volume against live consumers
The team did not have CI; the bug was merged without any automated checks of any kind
Float and double are stored identically in Parquet, so the change had no real effect — the incident was a false alarm
Catalyst auto-promotes float to double; the cast was effectively a no-op and the incident traced to something else
questions.correctAnswer: Tests used approximate equality; the precision loss only mattered at production volume against live consumers
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: tests with `assertAlmostEqual` masked the precision loss. Production volume amplified the per-row error into noticeable downstream divergence. Tests must use the same equality semantics as production.

questions.question: NordGrid's deployment lifecycle has many gates. What philosophy underlies them?
options: Each gate has explicit pass criteria and is fast; together they reduce blast radius without slowing routine work
Every gate must catch every possible bug; redundancy ensures no failure escapes through to production
Gates run only in production; pre-production environments are unmonitored to keep iteration fast
Gates are advisory; engineers decide whether to proceed regardless of the gate outcome
questions.correctAnswer: Each gate has explicit pass criteria and is fast; together they reduce blast radius without slowing routine work
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: gates trade a little speed for a lot of safety. Each gate is fast (seconds, not minutes) and tests one specific risk. Together they form a tight safety net that adds minutes, not hours, to a deployment.

questions.question: Spark's Unified Memory Model means execution and storage memory:
options: Share a pool — execution can evict storage at any time; storage can borrow free execution memory
Are physically separate JVM heaps that cannot share data between them at any point
Are merged into one undifferentiated region, so cache and shuffle compete for the same bytes with no policy
Are configured per-stage by Catalyst based on the operator type at plan compile time
questions.correctAnswer: Share a pool — execution can evict storage at any time; storage can borrow free execution memory
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: unified means dynamic sharing. Execution and storage have soft boundaries — execution evicts cache when it needs the space, storage uses spare execution memory when shuffles are idle.

questions.question: A PySpark UDF makes a job easier to OOM-kill at the CONTAINER level. Why?
options: Each executor spawns Python worker processes whose memory counts against the container limit but NOT the JVM heap
UDFs disable Spark's internal memory accounting, so spills can never occur on UDF-heavy stages
UDFs reserve 1 GB of executor memory each as a safety buffer for Python's reference counting
Python workers have a 4 GB hard limit that overrides the configured spark.executor.memory value
questions.correctAnswer: Each executor spawns Python worker processes whose memory counts against the container limit but NOT the JVM heap
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: the JVM heap is one part of the container. Python workers are separate processes whose memory is on top of the JVM. The container kill comes from total memory exceeding the YARN/K8s allocation, not the heap.

questions.question: When debugging an OOM error, what's the FIRST distinction to make?
options: JVM `OutOfMemoryError` (heap exhausted — increase heap or shrink data) vs container kill (overhead exceeded — increase memoryOverhead)
Driver vs executor OOM — the two have completely different fixes and mitigation strategies
Whether the data is Parquet or CSV — CSV inflates ~5× in memory and requires more headroom typically
Whether the OOM happened during read or write — write-side OOMs require coalesce, read-side require partition tuning
questions.correctAnswer: JVM `OutOfMemoryError` (heap exhausted — increase heap or shrink data) vs container kill (overhead exceeded — increase memoryOverhead)
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: the two error patterns look similar but have different fixes. JVM OOM = heap problem, fix with executor.memory. Container kill = overhead problem (Python, off-heap, native), fix with memoryOverhead.

questions.question: NordGrid caches a DataFrame ONLY when:
options: It's used by 2+ downstream actions AND recomputation cost exceeds 30 seconds
It's larger than 100 MB; smaller DataFrames are recomputed more cheaply than fetched from cache
It will be used at most once; caching once-used data is free given the spare cluster capacity
It contains string columns; string compression in cache is far better than in the underlying Parquet
questions.correctAnswer: It's used by 2+ downstream actions AND recomputation cost exceeds 30 seconds
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: caching has a cost (memory pressure, eviction work). It pays off only when the cached DataFrame is reused multiple times AND each recomputation is expensive. NordGrid uses 30s as the threshold.

questions.question: Default storage level for `df.cache()` is:
options: `MEMORY_AND_DISK` — try memory first, spill to local disk when execution memory pressure rises
`MEMORY_ONLY` — fastest possible reads; partitions that don't fit are silently dropped on overflow
`DISK_ONLY` — writes the whole DataFrame to disk immediately, no memory at all is used
`OFF_HEAP` — uses native (Tungsten) memory, bypassing the JVM heap entirely for storage
questions.correctAnswer: `MEMORY_AND_DISK` — try memory first, spill to local disk when execution memory pressure rises
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: MEMORY_AND_DISK is the safe default — fast for in-memory partitions, automatic disk spill for the rest. MEMORY_ONLY is faster but data loss is possible if the heap is too small.

questions.question: Forgetting to call `unpersist()` on a cached DataFrame causes:
options: Memory pressure that lasts the rest of the SparkSession; new caches evict useful entries unnecessarily
An immediate executor crash with a "cache leak" error that fails the next action call
The next action to recompute the DataFrame; cache entries auto-expire after one usage by design
A warning in logs but no functional impact; the GC eventually reclaims stale caches automatically
questions.correctAnswer: Memory pressure that lasts the rest of the SparkSession; new caches evict useful entries unnecessarily
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: cache lives until unpersist() or session end. Stale caches occupy memory the rest of the pipeline could use. New caches push them out, evicting useful entries instead.

questions.question: Broadcast VARIABLES (`sc.broadcast`) differ from broadcast JOINS in what way?
options: Variables broadcast any read-only value (dict, list, model); joins broadcast a DataFrame for an equi-join only
Variables run on the driver only; joins run on executors after the small side is shipped from driver
Variables are async; joins are blocking — that is the only difference between them at runtime
They are functionally equivalent — different APIs for the exact same underlying operation
questions.correctAnswer: Variables broadcast any read-only value (dict, list, model); joins broadcast a DataFrame for an equi-join only
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: broadcast joins are a Spark-API-level optimization for equi-joins. Broadcast variables are general-purpose: ship any Python object (dictionary, ML model, lookup table) to every executor for use inside transformations.

questions.question: A "Spill (Disk)" metric in the Spark UI Stages tab indicates:
options: Tasks ran out of execution memory and serialized working data to local disk during a sort or join
Cached DataFrames were evicted to disk because the storage region was full of other entries
Output files were written to disk as the final result of the stage (this is normal output activity)
The cluster's local SSDs were used for shuffle exchange, which is the standard happy path
questions.correctAnswer: Tasks ran out of execution memory and serialized working data to local disk during a sort or join
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: spill is a signal that working data exceeded execution memory. Spark wrote it to disk and re-read it later. Spill is not failure but indicates per-task data is too large; reduce data per partition or increase memory.

questions.question: `df.collect()` and `df.toPandas()` both bring data to the driver. Why dangerous?
options: Driver memory is fixed; bringing a multi-GB DataFrame to a 4 GB driver causes OOM with no graceful fallback
They run synchronously on a single executor, so they are slow but the operation itself is not unsafe
They temporarily disable AQE, which can lock the cluster into a bad partition layout for the rest of the run
They are deprecated; using them in Spark 3.x raises a runtime warning and falls back to take(20) silently
questions.correctAnswer: Driver memory is fixed; bringing a multi-GB DataFrame to a 4 GB driver causes OOM with no graceful fallback
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: the driver is one machine. collect() materializes the whole DataFrame in driver memory. On production-scale data, the driver OOMs before the data arrives. Always limit() or sample() first.

questions.question: NordGrid's quarterly memory tuning playbook works in what cycle?
options: Measure (90-day metrics) → Diagnose (bottleneck) → Fix (config or code) → Verify (re-measure)
Increase executor memory by 25% every quarter as data volume grows year-over-year naturally
Refactor the largest pipeline first; it is where the most memory wins are typically available
Roll out new memory settings to all pipelines simultaneously to keep configuration parity across them
questions.correctAnswer: Measure (90-day metrics) → Diagnose (bottleneck) → Fix (config or code) → Verify (re-measure)
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: tuning is a cycle, not a one-shot. Measure first to know the baseline, diagnose the actual bottleneck, fix one thing, verify the fix worked. Skipping any step risks regressing.

questions.question: NordGrid's "performance mindset" rule:
options: Never optimize without first identifying the bottleneck in the Spark UI — guesses regress as often as they help
Always increase executor memory first; it is the lowest-cost intervention with the broadest applicability
Optimize the longest stage first; shorter stages cannot yield meaningful improvement to total duration
Disable AQE during tuning so behaviour is deterministic and changes are easier to attribute
questions.correctAnswer: Never optimize without first identifying the bottleneck in the Spark UI — guesses regress as often as they help
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: optimization without diagnosis is gambling. The Spark UI tells you exactly which stage is slowest and why. Without that, you are guessing — and bad guesses regress as often as they help.

questions.question: To diagnose skew quickly, which Spark UI view is MOST informative?
options: Stage detail page → task duration histogram — a single bar far to the right means one task processed disproportionate data
Storage tab → cache fraction percentage — partial caches indicate skewed partition sizes by definition
Environment tab → JVM versions across executors — version mismatches cause processing skew between nodes
Executors tab → CPU utilization timeline — CPU peaks identify which executor is processing the heavy partition
questions.correctAnswer: Stage detail page → task duration histogram — a single bar far to the right means one task processed disproportionate data
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: the histogram visualizes the long-tail signature of skew immediately. Most tasks finish in similar time; one task is far to the right. That visual catches skew faster than any other view.

questions.question: The SQL/DataFrame tab shows estimated vs actual row counts per operator. A 10× discrepancy suggests:
options: Stale or missing statistics — the CBO may have made suboptimal join strategy decisions based on bad estimates
A bug in Catalyst — file a Spark issue with the explain() output and reproducer attached for triage
AQE is disabled — runtime statistics normally close the gap between compile-time estimates and reality
Heavy GC — the JVM's memory bookkeeping is interfering with the row-count metric instrumentation
questions.correctAnswer: Stale or missing statistics — the CBO may have made suboptimal join strategy decisions based on bad estimates
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: a big estimate-vs-actual gap means the CBO worked from bad data. The join strategy it chose may be wrong. Run ANALYZE TABLE to refresh statistics, or rely on AQE which uses runtime measurements.

questions.question: An executor's GC time exceeds 15% of total task time. This typically means:
options: The heap is nearly full — the JVM spends more time managing memory than processing data; increase memory or shrink working set
The executor is healthy — high GC is normal for sort-merge joins and other shuffle-heavy operations
Catalyst is regenerating plans on every task — disable AQE to stop this redundant overhead immediately
The cluster has more executors than needed — scale down to reduce per-executor GC overhead noticeably
questions.correctAnswer: The heap is nearly full — the JVM spends more time managing memory than processing data; increase memory or shrink working set
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: 15% GC time is the warning threshold. The JVM is fighting to free space by repeatedly running collection. Either give the executor more memory or reduce per-task data via more partitions.

questions.question: Why instrument pipelines with a metrics table even though Spark UI shows the same data?
options: Spark UI vanishes when the application terminates; the metrics table persists for time-series and regression detection
Spark UI only shows the last 100 jobs; long pipelines lose history beyond that limit quickly
The metrics table is faster to query than the Spark UI's REST API for ad-hoc analytical queries
Spark UI requires admin access; the metrics table can be read by anyone with database privileges
questions.correctAnswer: Spark UI vanishes when the application terminates; the metrics table persists for time-series and regression detection
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: persistent metrics enable trend analysis. The Spark UI is great for one run, but to detect "this pipeline has gradually slowed over 3 months" you need a time-series table that outlives the SparkSession.

questions.question: A shuffle with high "Shuffle Read" but balanced task durations indicates:
options: Volume issue, not skew — the shuffle is large but fairly distributed; reduce volume via filter / prune before shuffle
Skew — duration is balanced because Spark optimizes around it via AQE silently in the background
Memory pressure — even balanced shuffles spill when partition sizes exceed available execution memory
A Spark bug — balanced durations should always come with a low total shuffle read by design
questions.correctAnswer: Volume issue, not skew — the shuffle is large but fairly distributed; reduce volume via filter / prune before shuffle
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: balanced + high shuffle read = volume problem. Skew would show imbalanced durations. The fix is reducing volume, not redistributing it — pre-filter or prune columns above the shuffle.

questions.question: A scan-bound stage with `bytes_read` close to the full table size means:
options: Predicate pushdown and column pruning are not working — every byte is being read; check plan for missing PartitionFilters
The Parquet files are corrupted — Spark falls back to reading whole files when corruption is detected
The data is well-compressed and being decompressed on-the-fly during the scan as expected
The cluster has too few executors — files queue up waiting for compute capacity to free up
questions.correctAnswer: Predicate pushdown and column pruning are not working — every byte is being read; check plan for missing PartitionFilters
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: full-table scan when you expected partition pruning means the filter isn't being pushed. Check the plan for PartitionFilters and ReadSchema. Common cause: a UDF or a non-partition-column filter.

questions.question: NordGrid adjusts properties in a specific ORDER when tuning a pipeline. The principle?
options: Free, high-impact first (`shuffle.partitions`, `autoBroadcastJoinThreshold`); resource changes (memory, executors) only after
Largest impact first regardless of cost — speed-of-fix is the only criterion when tuning under time pressure
Resource changes first because they are the most predictable interventions across diverse pipelines
Random order — Spark's tuning is too non-linear to follow a fixed sequence reliably across cases
questions.correctAnswer: Free, high-impact first (`shuffle.partitions`, `autoBroadcastJoinThreshold`); resource changes (memory, executors) only after
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: tune the cheap knobs first. Free changes (configs) take seconds and can cut runtimes in half. Resource changes (more memory, more executors) cost real money — only escalate when free options are exhausted.

questions.question: A pipeline gradually slowed from 7 to 14 minutes over 3 months. What detects this BEFORE an SLA breach?
options: Time-series regression detection on duration / shuffle / spill — a moving baseline that alerts on slow drift
Daily Slack reports of every pipeline's duration — engineers spot trends visually in their morning standup
A flaky test that fires on slow runs — coincidental detection through unrelated infrastructure noise
Hardware monitoring — slow pipelines correlate with disk wear; replace disks proactively to prevent it
questions.correctAnswer: Time-series regression detection on duration / shuffle / spill — a moving baseline that alerts on slow drift
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: gradual degradation is invisible to per-run alerts. Each individual run is "fine". A trend on persisted metrics catches the slow drift before it crosses the SLA threshold.

questions.question: NordGrid's three-level escalation path for SLA breaches starts with:
options: Level 1 (automated): Slack alert from regression detection — engineers diagnose using the Spark UI within 30 minutes
Level 1 (page): on-call gets paged for any duration over the SLA, no matter how minor the breach is
Level 1 (rollback): the previous artifact is auto-deployed; investigation happens after recovery is complete
Level 1 (skip): pipelines that exceed SLA are skipped that day to keep the rest of the cluster healthy
questions.correctAnswer: Level 1 (automated): Slack alert from regression detection — engineers diagnose using the Spark UI within 30 minutes
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: Level 1 is informational, not disruptive. The team gets a Slack alert and investigates. Pages and rollbacks are reserved for higher levels when the breach impacts downstream consumers.

questions.question: NordGrid runs BOTH batch and streaming pipelines, not one or the other. Why?
options: Batch produces the authoritative Silver record; streaming gives near-real-time visibility — they serve different consumers
Streaming is unstable, so batch is needed as a fallback; one would suffice if streaming were truly reliable
Batch is for development, streaming is for production — they are never both deployed in the same environment
Catalyst cannot compile a streaming query without a batch query for it to inherit semantics from at runtime
questions.correctAnswer: Batch produces the authoritative Silver record; streaming gives near-real-time visibility — they serve different consumers
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: each serves a distinct purpose. Batch handles correctness (reconciliation, compliance, full-history queries). Streaming handles latency (operational dashboards, alerts). Different consumers, different SLAs.

questions.question: A streaming DataFrame REQUIRES an explicit schema. Why?
options: A streaming source has no complete dataset to scan when the query starts — Spark needs the schema before any data arrives
Catalyst's optimizer cannot compile streaming queries without a schema literal hardcoded in the source code
Inference is too slow for streaming workloads; explicit schemas are 10× faster to load on the first batch
Spark's streaming API was designed before inference existed and has not been updated to support it since
questions.correctAnswer: A streaming source has no complete dataset to scan when the query starts — Spark needs the schema before any data arrives
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: inference works in batch because Spark can scan the file. In streaming, the data is unbounded — inference would require seeing the future. The schema must be supplied up front.

questions.question: For a watermarked groupBy aggregation, APPEND mode emits results when:
options: The window is FINALIZED — the watermark passes the window's end time, so no more late data is expected
Every micro-batch — append mode is real-time and emits incrementally on every trigger boundary
When manually triggered by the user via `query.flush()` or a similar explicit emit operation
After 1000 rows accumulate in the partial aggregate buffer for that window key combination
questions.correctAnswer: The window is FINALIZED — the watermark passes the window's end time, so no more late data is expected
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: append + watermark = result emitted once per window, when finalized. The watermark provides the "no more late data" guarantee that lets append output the result without risk of revising it later.

questions.question: A streaming query's CHECKPOINT location stores:
options: Query progress — which files were processed, which offsets were consumed, intermediate aggregation state
The output of the query — a complete copy of all results so far, replayable on restart from any point
Catalyst's compiled physical plan, so the query restarts faster on recovery without re-compilation overhead
Authentication tokens for the source system, so the restart can resume reading without re-authenticating
questions.correctAnswer: Query progress — which files were processed, which offsets were consumed, intermediate aggregation state
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: checkpoint = streaming query state. Without it, a restart cannot know where to resume. With it, the query picks up exactly where it left off — no duplicates, no skipped data.

questions.question: `maxFilesPerTrigger` on a file-streaming source controls:
options: How many new files each micro-batch processes — bounds per-batch work so backlogs do not overwhelm the cluster
The maximum number of files the source directory can contain before Spark refuses to read it any further
The retention window for processed file metadata; beyond this files can be re-read again
The minimum file size threshold; files smaller than this are skipped to avoid the small-files problem
questions.correctAnswer: How many new files each micro-batch processes — bounds per-batch work so backlogs do not overwhelm the cluster
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: maxFilesPerTrigger throttles the per-batch work. A query that wakes up to 1000 backlogged files would otherwise try to process all 1000 — likely OOM or timeout. The cap keeps each micro-batch bounded.

questions.question: Three operations work in BATCH but NOT on streaming DataFrames. Which is one of them?
options: `sort()` / `orderBy()` — sorting requires seeing all rows, impossible on unbounded streaming data
`filter()` — streaming filters require a watermark column to bound the state size of the predicate
`withColumn()` — column derivations need cached input rows for streaming sources at runtime
`select()` — requires a full schema, which streaming sources do not define until the first batch
questions.correctAnswer: `sort()` / `orderBy()` — sorting requires seeing all rows, impossible on unbounded streaming data
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: sort/distinct/limit don't work on streams because they need the complete dataset. filter/select/withColumn work fine — they're per-row operations that don't care about the full row set.

questions.question: A watermark of `'30 minutes'` on a window aggregation means:
options: Spark waits 30 minutes past the maximum event timestamp seen so far before finalizing a window
The watermark itself is set every 30 minutes; in between, no late data is accepted into windows
Late data older than 30 minutes is dropped; data within 30 minutes is processed normally afterwards
Output is delayed by 30 minutes regardless of event time — pure throttling unrelated to data lateness
questions.correctAnswer: Spark waits 30 minutes past the maximum event timestamp seen so far before finalizing a window
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: the watermark advances with the event time stream. When the watermark passes a window's end + lateness threshold, Spark closes the window and emits the result. Late data after that is dropped.

questions.question: NordGrid joins a STREAMING readings DataFrame with a STATIC meters DataFrame. What happens to the static side?
options: Read once when the query starts and cached in executor memory; every micro-batch joins against it
Re-read for every micro-batch to capture the latest state — eventual-consistency semantics on the join
Pushed into the streaming source so the join physically happens at read time on the source partition
Replicated to a Delta sidecar so updates propagate to the streaming join within seconds of writing
questions.correctAnswer: Read once when the query starts and cached in executor memory; every micro-batch joins against it
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: stream-static join = static side cached at start. To pick up updates, restart the query (the checkpoint preserves stream progress). NordGrid restarts daily after the batch refresh.

questions.question: Backpressure in a streaming query means:
options: Data arrives faster than the query can process it — input rate exceeds processing rate consistently
The query has hit a JVM memory limit and is GC-thrashing instead of processing rows
A downstream consumer rejected a write and is requesting retransmission of the failed batch
The cluster manager throttled the query to save resources for higher-priority jobs in the queue
questions.correctAnswer: Data arrives faster than the query can process it — input rate exceeds processing rate consistently
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: backpressure = falling behind. Each micro-batch processes some data, but more arrives before it finishes. The lag grows over time. Detected by comparing inputRowsPerSecond vs processedRowsPerSecond.

questions.question: NordGrid's unified architecture has both batch and streaming WRITE TO SEPARATE Delta tables. Why?
options: Batch is authoritative (daily reconciliation, compliance); streaming is near-real-time (operational dashboards) — neither must overwrite the other
Streaming cannot write to Delta tables that already exist; it must always create its own new table on first run
The batch table uses a different file format (ORC) than the streaming table (Delta) for performance reasons
Spark's planner does not allow concurrent writers to the same Delta table from different applications
questions.correctAnswer: Batch is authoritative (daily reconciliation, compliance); streaming is near-real-time (operational dashboards) — neither must overwrite the other
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: the two tables serve different consumers. Batch is the source of truth for reports; streaming is the source of "what's happening now" for dashboards. Mixing them would compromise both purposes.

questions.question: GridUnion's "make the default path the correct path" principle means:
options: Platform conventions (naming, ownership, contracts) are designed so the correct behavior is the easiest behavior
Every pipeline must follow the platform team's explicit code review before merging to the main branch
Production deployments are blocked until two senior engineers approve via Slack reactions
New tables are blocked from the catalog until a quarterly governance committee approves the design
questions.correctAnswer: Platform conventions (naming, ownership, contracts) are designed so the correct behavior is the easiest behavior
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: at scale, you can't police every decision. The platform encodes correctness into defaults — naming conventions enforce schema isolation, the CI template adds quality gates, the namespace prevents collisions automatically.

questions.question: GridUnion picked the environment-first catalog hierarchy (`dev.silver_de.readings`) over team-first because:
options: Environment promotion is the most frequent operation; same schema across catalogs lets dev/staging/prod share table names
Team-first hierarchies are forbidden by Unity Catalog's permission model under any conditions
Environment-first is the only way to share data between teams without copying tables across catalogs
Spark requires the catalog name to match the cluster's deployment environment for proper routing
questions.correctAnswer: Environment promotion is the most frequent operation; same schema across catalogs lets dev/staging/prod share table names
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: optimize for the most-frequent operation. Promotion happens daily; team boundaries change yearly. Environment-first means a deploy is "swap the catalog" — table paths stay the same.

questions.question: GridUnion enforces "single ownership" per table. The reason is:
options: When everyone can write, nobody is responsible — single ownership prevents the tragedy of the commons
Unity Catalog only supports one writer per table; multi-writer support is not yet released
Single ownership is a Spark requirement for Delta tables to maintain ACID transaction guarantees
It reduces metastore I/O — multiple owners would require multiple catalog updates per write
questions.correctAnswer: When everyone can write, nobody is responsible — single ownership prevents the tragedy of the commons
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: the producer-consumer contract requires a clear producer. Without single ownership, schema decisions are ad-hoc, quality declines, and incidents have no responsible team.

questions.question: GridUnion adds a fourth Platinum layer above Gold ONLY when:
options: Multiple Gold tables from different domains must be joined for a use case AND the join is repeated by 3+ consumers
The Gold layer is too slow to query directly; Platinum is a materialized cache for performance reasons
A regulator requires a separate audit-ready layer; Platinum is mandatory for compliance frameworks like SOX
Streaming pipelines need their own layer because batch and streaming cannot share storage paths
questions.correctAnswer: Multiple Gold tables from different domains must be joined for a use case AND the join is repeated by 3+ consumers
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: layers should match ownership boundaries, not analytical convenience. Platinum is justified when cross-domain joins repeat across consumers — once is materialization, three+ is architectural reuse.

questions.question: A "data product" differs from "a table" by including:
options: A declared schema, freshness SLA, quality contract, owner team, and consumer registration in a manifest
A complete copy of the data in compressed binary form alongside the metadata file in the catalog
A versioned snapshot taken every hour, retained for 30 days under regulatory compliance requirements
An automatically-generated dashboard tied to the table's freshness via the platform monitoring system
questions.correctAnswer: A declared schema, freshness SLA, quality contract, owner team, and consumer registration in a manifest
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: a table is technical (files + schema). A data product is organizational (table + commitments + accountability). The manifest is the artifact that captures the difference.

questions.question: Cross-team pipeline dependencies are tracked in a graph. Why is detecting CYCLES critical?
options: Cycles cause deadlocks where pipeline A waits for B, B for C, C for A — none ever runs successfully
Cycles consume more storage than acyclic dependencies because each pipeline reads its own outputs
Cycles violate Unity Catalog's permission model and prevent the catalog from registering the tables
Cycles trigger Spark's circular reference detector which kills the entire application on submission
questions.correctAnswer: Cycles cause deadlocks where pipeline A waits for B, B for C, C for A — none ever runs successfully
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: in a single-team Airflow DAG, cycles are detected at definition time. Across teams with separate DAGs, cycles only manifest at runtime as "everyone waiting for everyone." The dependency graph catches it pre-deployment.

questions.question: GridUnion's configuration "resolution order" is shared defaults → environment → team → pipeline. Why this layering?
options: Each layer overrides the next, letting platform standards apply by default while teams override only what differs
Spark loads configurations in this exact order; reversing it causes resource allocation to fail at startup
It enables a unique per-environment encryption key that protects pipeline-specific secrets between layers
The resolution order is required by GitOps tooling — alternative orderings break the merge algorithm
questions.correctAnswer: Each layer overrides the next, letting platform standards apply by default while teams override only what differs
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: layered config = sane defaults + selective overrides. Most pipelines inherit platform standards; pipeline-specific overrides only exist where genuinely needed. Each level is auditable.

questions.question: Cost chargeback isn't just billing — it's also a:
options: Governance mechanism that changes team behavior once costs become visible (e.g. switch to spot instances)
Mandatory regulatory requirement under SOX for any cluster shared by more than three teams in production
Performance optimization tool — Spark uses chargeback metrics to balance workloads across YARN queues
Required input to the Catalyst optimizer's cost model for choosing join strategies between teams
questions.correctAnswer: Governance mechanism that changes team behavior once costs become visible (e.g. switch to spot instances)
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: cost visibility changes behavior. When the ML team saw their $35K → $54K spike, they switched to spot instances and lifecycle-purged old features. Hidden costs don't self-optimize.

questions.question: Platform "observability" differs from "monitoring" by answering:
options: "Why did this pipeline fail and what else was affected?" — not just "is the pipeline running?"
"How fast is the average pipeline?" — only the population statistics, not per-pipeline detail
"Which executor has the most CPU?" — observability is exclusively about resource utilization metrics
"What's the cluster manager's queue depth?" — observability is the cluster-level view of work
questions.correctAnswer: "Why did this pipeline fail and what else was affected?" — not just "is the pipeline running?"
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: monitoring tells you what's broken. Observability tells you why and what else is downstream. The four signals (freshness, quality, cost, runtime) plus lineage make root-cause analysis routine.

questions.question: GridUnion's governance committee meets QUARTERLY, not weekly or daily. Why?
options: Architectural decisions need stability — too-frequent changes prevent teams from depending on standards
YARN queue allocations only support quarterly reconfiguration; weekly changes would crash the scheduler
Unity Catalog's metastore caches policies for 90 days, so quarterly is the maximum useful cadence
The platform's Spark version upgrade cycle is exactly quarterly, anchoring the governance rhythm
questions.correctAnswer: Architectural decisions need stability — too-frequent changes prevent teams from depending on standards
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: governance is durable decisions. Quarterly cadence balances responsiveness with stability — fast enough to react to platform-wide problems, slow enough that teams can build on top of the standards.

questions.question: Catalyst rules are organized into BATCHES with FixedPoint or Once iteration. What does FixedPoint do?
options: Applies all rules in the batch repeatedly until the plan tree stops changing (or hits maxIterations)
Runs the rules in a fixed sequence exactly once, then moves to the next batch in deterministic order
Caches the rule application result for the rest of the session, replaying it on subsequent queries
Locks the plan tree at a specific node and prevents downstream rules from modifying anything above
questions.correctAnswer: Applies all rules in the batch repeatedly until the plan tree stops changing (or hits maxIterations)
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: FixedPoint = iterate to convergence. Rules within a batch can enable each other (filter pushdown might enable column pruning). Iterating until stable lets rules cooperate without explicit ordering.

questions.question: A 5-join pipeline with 2× error at each join produces what cumulative estimation error?
options: 32× — error compounds multiplicatively through plan depth: 2^5 = 32
10× — error scales linearly with join count: 5 × 2 = 10
2× — Catalyst clamps cumulative error to the maximum single-join error to avoid divergence
1× — AQE corrects all upstream errors at runtime, so cumulative error is always negligible
questions.correctAnswer: 32× — error compounds multiplicatively through plan depth: 2^5 = 32
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: estimation errors compound through plan depth. Each join multiplies the upstream error. A 5-join pipeline with realistic 2× per-join error is off by 32× at the output — which is why CBO decisions on deep plans are unreliable.

questions.question: A 200-row reference table joins a 3 TB fact table. CBO picks SortMergeJoin instead of broadcast. Most likely:
options: No statistics on the reference table — without `ANALYZE TABLE`, CBO cannot estimate the size accurately
The fact table's file footprint exceeds 16 GB, which forces SortMergeJoin regardless of small-side size
The CBO weights network cost above all other factors, and broadcast incurs network cost during distribution
AQE was disabled for this query, and SortMergeJoin is the safe fallback when AQE cannot adapt at runtime
questions.correctAnswer: No statistics on the reference table — without `ANALYZE TABLE`, CBO cannot estimate the size accurately
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: CBO needs stats. Without them, every table looks "potentially large" and the safe choice is SortMergeJoin. Hint with `broadcast()` is information injection — telling the CBO what stats lack.

questions.question: AQE's value over CBO is that AQE:
options: Uses ACTUAL runtime data sizes (post-shuffle) instead of compile-time estimates that may be stale
Replaces Catalyst entirely after the first stage, eliminating rule-based optimization overhead
Runs only at compile time but uses higher-quality statistics from cached system catalogs
Disables predicate pushdown to avoid double-filtering at the source and runtime layers
questions.correctAnswer: Uses ACTUAL runtime data sizes (post-shuffle) instead of compile-time estimates that may be stale
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: AQE's superpower is real measurements. Compile-time stats can be stale or wrong; AQE's decisions (coalesce, broadcast conversion, skew split) use what actually happened.

questions.question: Custom Catalyst rules are powerful but DANGEROUS because:
options: They modify the plan of every matching query across every pipeline — a buggy rule silently corrupts results platform-wide
They run before AQE, so any error halts the entire query before AQE can compensate at runtime
They require recompiling Spark from source; misconfiguration prevents the cluster from starting at all
They bypass Unity Catalog's permission checks, exposing tables that should remain restricted
questions.correctAnswer: They modify the plan of every matching query across every pipeline — a buggy rule silently corrupts results platform-wide
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: custom rules sit below the API surface. Code reviewers don't see them in PRs. A rule that fires on the wrong pattern affects every team. Treat them as platform infrastructure with extreme rigor.

questions.question: GridUnion fingerprints physical plans to detect regressions across Spark upgrades. What does the fingerprint capture?
options: Key plan characteristics: chosen join strategies, scan operators, broadcast presence — compact enough to compare across versions
Every byte of the plan tree as a SHA-256 hash; any change at all triggers an alert for engineer review
Only the operator names, ignoring partition counts and data sizes which vary across runs naturally
The estimated row counts at every node, allowing CBO regression detection without examining the structure
questions.correctAnswer: Key plan characteristics: chosen join strategies, scan operators, broadcast presence — compact enough to compare across versions
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: fingerprints are intentional reductions. They capture decisions you care about (broadcast vs sort-merge) and ignore decisions you don't (cosmetic plan reorderings). Right granularity is the design challenge.

questions.question: A `*(2)` annotation on a physical plan operator means:
options: The operator is fused with others into a single Java method (codegen) — eliminating per-row dispatch overhead
The operator has an estimated cost of 2 units in the CBO's cost model relative to the cheapest alternative
The operator is the second to execute in the stage, scheduled after the first whole-stage barrier
Two separate executors process the operator in parallel for redundancy and fault tolerance reasons
questions.correctAnswer: The operator is fused with others into a single Java method (codegen) — eliminating per-row dispatch overhead
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: whole-stage codegen fuses operator chains into one tight loop. The number indicates the stage; same number = same fused method. This is Tungsten's answer to the abstraction penalty of modular operators.

questions.question: Predicate pushdown is described as a "negotiation, not a guarantee." What does that mean?
options: Catalyst PROPOSES predicates to the data source; the source ACCEPTS or REJECTS each one based on its capabilities
Pushdown depends on the cluster's network bandwidth at the moment — high latency disables pushdown silently
Catalyst always pushes the same predicates, but the source ignores them randomly to balance load
Pushdown only happens for the first 100 predicates in a query; beyond that, Catalyst gives up on the negotiation
questions.correctAnswer: Catalyst PROPOSES predicates to the data source; the source ACCEPTS or REJECTS each one based on its capabilities
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: pushdown is a contract between optimizer and data source. Parquet supports many filter types; JDBC supports fewer. Verifying PushedFilters in the plan tells you what was actually accepted.

questions.question: For a SCADA telemetry table with a 200-field nested struct column, what improves query performance most?
options: Flattening the struct into 200 top-level columns — predicate and column pushdown work better on flat schemas
Adding more partitions to the existing struct column to enable partition pruning at the field level
Compressing the struct column with ZSTD instead of Snappy to reduce I/O during nested field access
Caching the table with MEMORY_AND_DISK so subsequent queries skip the struct decoding overhead entirely
questions.correctAnswer: Flattening the struct into 200 top-level columns — predicate and column pushdown work better on flat schemas
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: nested column pruning has limitations. For wide nested schemas, flattening is the highest-leverage optimization — pushdown and pruning operate cleanly on flat columns.

questions.question: GridUnion's optimizer-incident retrospective adds questions BEYOND the standard post-mortem. Why?
options: Optimizer issues have specific recovery actions (refresh stats, change hints, custom rule) different from generic incidents
Optimizer incidents are treated as low-severity and bypass the standard post-mortem process entirely
The optimizer team operates in a separate organization with its own retrospective format and tools
Optimizer incidents always recur, so the retrospective focuses purely on documenting the workaround
questions.correctAnswer: Optimizer issues have specific recovery actions (refresh stats, change hints, custom rule) different from generic incidents
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: generic post-mortems ask "what happened?". Optimizer post-mortems ask "what stats were stale?", "what plan changed?", "what hint should we add?" — domain-specific recovery questions.

questions.question: Partition design for multi-consumer tables uses a SCORING MATRIX instead of intuition. Why?
options: Multiple objectives compete (filter frequency, selectivity, cardinality) — the matrix forces explicit weighting and traceable decisions
Spark's partition planner reads the matrix file directly during query compilation to optimize layouts
It is required by Unity Catalog's catalog-creation API for any table partitioned by more than one column
The matrix automatically generates the partitionBy code; bypassing it leads to syntactically invalid Spark code
questions.correctAnswer: Multiple objectives compete (filter frequency, selectivity, cardinality) — the matrix forces explicit weighting and traceable decisions
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: at scale you can't please all 12 consumers equally. The matrix exposes trade-offs (one team gets less selective pruning so another gets directory isolation). Documents the decision for future review.

questions.question: GridUnion's partition sizing model PROJECTS forward 2 years. The reason:
options: A scheme that's right today may be wrong as data grows; project ahead to avoid designing for current volume only
Spark's default partition tuner requires a 24-month projection input to choose the initial partition count
Cloud providers offer 2-year reservation discounts only when capacity is forecasted in matching units
Regulatory reporting requires data that will exist in 2 years to be partitioned consistently with current data
questions.correctAnswer: A scheme that's right today may be wrong as data grows; project ahead to avoid designing for current volume only
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: partitioning is hard to change later (lesson 8). Designing for current data means redesigning every 18 months. Projecting forward catches scaling cliffs (directory count, files-per-directory) before they hit.

questions.question: Z-ORDER differs from partitioning by:
options: Organizing data WITHIN files (file-level skipping) vs partitioning organizes data INTO directories (directory-level skipping)
Operating only on numeric columns; partitioning works on any data type without restriction
Replacing partitioning entirely; tables that use Z-ORDER cannot have any partition columns at all
Running at query time (just-in-time clustering); partitioning is fixed at write time and cannot be changed
questions.correctAnswer: Organizing data WITHIN files (file-level skipping) vs partitioning organizes data INTO directories (directory-level skipping)
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: two granularities of skipping. Partitioning skips directories (coarse, low-cardinality columns). Z-ORDER skips files within a directory (fine, medium-cardinality columns). They're complementary, not alternatives.

questions.question: GridUnion's "silent DPP failure" was caused by:
options: A condition required for DPP activation was missing (e.g. partition column mismatch); DPP silently disabled itself
The dimension table grew above the broadcast threshold; DPP activated but materialized to local disk first
A custom optimizer rule (PSS2) accidentally fired on the join pattern and removed the DPP annotation
The Spark version upgrade changed DPP defaults; the team wasn't aware DPP needed re-enabling per session
questions.correctAnswer: A condition required for DPP activation was missing (e.g. partition column mismatch); DPP silently disabled itself
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: DPP's 4 conditions all must be met. Miss one (e.g. join key isn't the partition column on fact, or dimension grows past broadcast threshold) and DPP silently turns off — no error, just slow scans.

questions.question: Small files at enterprise scale are dangerous because cloud object stores:
options: Charge per LIST/GET request; millions of small files multiply the metadata operation cost dramatically
Replicate every file 3× across regions, so small files inflate cross-region replication bandwidth costs
Refuse to serve files smaller than 1 MB to prevent DOS attacks against the storage backend service
Cache files larger than 100 MB but not smaller ones; small files always miss the storage tier cache
questions.correctAnswer: Charge per LIST/GET request; millions of small files multiply the metadata operation cost dramatically
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: cloud object stores are designed for large objects. Each file open is a LIST + GET cycle billed per request. Million-file directories pay both in latency (sequential metadata ops) and dollars.

questions.question: Bucketing at scale is described as "infrastructure decision, not pipeline decision." Why?
options: Once a bucket key/count is established, every table joined on that key must use matching buckets — coupling pipelines
Bucketing requires platform-team approval per Unity Catalog policy before any pipeline can use bucketed tables
Buckets occupy reserved storage in the cluster's shared pool, costing money even when no pipeline reads them
Bucketing is configured at the YARN queue level and can't be changed without restarting the cluster manager
questions.correctAnswer: Once a bucket key/count is established, every table joined on that key must use matching buckets — coupling pipelines
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: bucketing creates cross-table dependencies. Choose meter_id with 16 buckets and EVERY table joined on meter_id must match. Adding a 17th-bucket table later breaks shuffle-free joins everywhere.

questions.question: Repartitioning a live 8 TB table that 12 consumers depend on, with 90-day deadline, what's the LOWEST-RISK approach?
options: Dual-write: write every batch to both old and new tables in parallel; consumers migrate one-by-one before retiring old
Direct overwrite: stop all consumers, repartition in place during one weekend, restart consumers Monday morning
Schema evolution: add the new partition columns as virtual columns and let Catalyst auto-route at read time
CRAS without coordination: rewrite the table with the new scheme; consumers will discover the new layout naturally
questions.correctAnswer: Dual-write: write every batch to both old and new tables in parallel; consumers migrate one-by-one before retiring old
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: dual-write has zero downtime. The old table continues to work; consumers migrate at their own pace. The trade-off is double storage during migration. CRAS is faster but downtime; direct overwrite is reckless.

questions.question: GridUnion's "partition health dashboard" runs every 6 hours, not daily. Why?
options: Partition health degrades gradually; sub-daily monitoring catches drift before nightly compaction would address it
Spark's metadata cache expires every 6 hours; refreshing the dashboard reloads the cached partition statistics
Cloud storage charges drop after 6 hours of file inactivity, making the dashboard cheaper at this cadence
The platform team's on-call rotation is 6-hour shifts; each shift starts by reviewing the latest dashboard
questions.correctAnswer: Partition health degrades gradually; sub-daily monitoring catches drift before nightly compaction would address it
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: the feedback loop must be tighter than the rate of degradation. Streaming appends create small files in minutes; once-daily monitoring misses the buildup. 6 hours catches issues mid-day.

questions.question: GridUnion's partition-design review is described as "the cheapest form of partition optimization." Why?
options: A 30-minute review prevents the 6-week repartitioning project that's required to fix a wrong partition scheme
The review eliminates the need for ANALYZE TABLE; partition design implicitly creates accurate statistics
Spark's automatic partition tuner cannot be applied without a written design document for input
The platform reimburses the team's cloud cost for any pipeline that completed a design review
questions.correctAnswer: A 30-minute review prevents the 6-week repartitioning project that's required to fix a wrong partition scheme
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: prevention vs cure. Repartitioning a live table is among the most expensive data engineering tasks. A short review caught early prevents that downstream pain.

questions.question: Why does "the best repartitioning strategy is the one you never need to execute" apply at GridUnion scale?
options: Live repartitioning of multi-TB tables is high-risk and weeks of work; upfront design pays for itself many times
Repartitioning is forbidden by Spark's default cluster policy; tables must be designed correctly the first time
Cloud providers charge a special tariff for repartitioning workloads that exceeds normal compute costs by 10×
Repartitioning rebuilds Unity Catalog's privilege graph; teams lose access until the catalog refreshes manually
questions.correctAnswer: Live repartitioning of multi-TB tables is high-risk and weeks of work; upfront design pays for itself many times
questions.explanation: Lesson 8 closing principle: repartitioning is expensive. Hours of upfront design beats weeks of migration project. Invest in getting it right the first time.

questions.question: At senior scale, the unit of join optimization shifts from individual joins to:
options: The join TOPOLOGY — star schema, multi-hop chain, snowflake — each calls for different optimization strategies
The cluster's YARN queue assignment, which determines available resources for any join in any pipeline
The Unity Catalog table classification (Bronze/Silver/Gold), which dictates allowable join strategies per layer
The Spark version installed, since join performance varies dramatically across minor versions of the engine
questions.correctAnswer: The join TOPOLOGY — star schema, multi-hop chain, snowflake — each calls for different optimization strategies
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: a star-schema join is independent per dimension; a multi-hop chain compounds errors and intermediate volume. Same join SYNTAX, completely different optimization plays.

questions.question: When a bucketed table's schema changes (new column), the bucket alignment problem is:
options: Adding a column requires rewriting files; the rewrite must preserve the original bucket partitioning exactly
Spark automatically re-buckets the table on first read after the schema change; this is a one-time cost
Adding a column is forbidden on bucketed tables; you must drop and recreate the table from scratch
New columns appear in extra "bucket overflow" files that bypass the bucket structure for the new column
questions.correctAnswer: Adding a column requires rewriting files; the rewrite must preserve the original bucket partitioning exactly
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: bucketed files encode the bucket layout. Adding a column rewrites the files — the new write must use the same bucket count and column. One slip and the join falls back to shuffle.

questions.question: A range join (`event_time BETWEEN start AND end`) on two large tables. What's the practical mitigation?
options: Bin-based approximation: discretize the range into time bins and convert to an equality join on bin_id
Force a SortMergeJoin via hint; range conditions automatically engage Spark's optimized range merge path
Replace the range with separate equality conditions on start and end; Catalyst combines them efficiently
Convert to streaming with a watermark; range joins only work efficiently on streaming DataFrames in Spark 3+
questions.correctAnswer: Bin-based approximation: discretize the range into time bins and convert to an equality join on bin_id
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: range joins fall back to nested loops. Discretizing into bins (5-min buckets) lets you join on bin_id (equality, hash-based). Then a residual filter handles bin-edge cases.

questions.question: Catalyst's "join elimination" rule REMOVES a join when:
options: No column from the eliminated table appears in SELECT/WHERE/GROUP BY/ORDER BY (except the join key for matching)
The eliminated table is smaller than the autoBroadcastJoinThreshold; the join cost exceeds its benefit
AQE detects at runtime that the join produces zero rows; it short-circuits the rest of the query plan
The eliminated table has no statistics; CBO conservatively skips joins it can't cost-estimate accurately
questions.correctAnswer: No column from the eliminated table appears in SELECT/WHERE/GROUP BY/ORDER BY (except the join key for matching)
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: if no columns from a join contribute to the output, the join was unused. Catalyst proves this and removes it. Wide views with selective consumers benefit hugely — joins disappear automatically.

questions.question: Bloom-filter pushdown vs DPP — what's the granularity difference?
options: DPP skips at the partition-directory level (coarse); Bloom filters skip at the row-group/file level (fine)
Bloom filters skip per-row; DPP skips entire stages of the plan based on optimizer estimates
They are equivalent — Bloom filter is the new name for DPP starting in Spark 3.5 onwards
DPP works only on partitioned tables; Bloom filters work only on bucketed tables (mutually exclusive)
questions.correctAnswer: DPP skips at the partition-directory level (coarse); Bloom filters skip at the row-group/file level (fine)
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: complementary, not redundant. DPP cuts directories; Bloom filters cut Parquet row groups within unpruned directories. Both can apply to the same query.

questions.question: GridUnion treats skew as an "architectural problem at the data design level," not just a runtime issue. The implication:
options: Sometimes the right fix is redesigning the join key (composite key) instead of salting at every query site
Skew is solved by hardware — adding executors with more memory absorbs imbalance without code changes
Catalyst includes a built-in skew detector that auto-rewrites queries; manual intervention is rarely needed
Skew is a Spark version regression that resolves itself with the next Spark upgrade, no architectural action
questions.correctAnswer: Sometimes the right fix is redesigning the join key (composite key) instead of salting at every query site
